rebase'd  for tidier history :-/

A use case for this change that I find especially compelling:

It would make it substantially easier to make use of a custom response that gets serialized (in middleware) as a function of request headers. (E.g., based on a request header, serialize to json, msgpack, protobuf, etc.). Currently that would require you to change the response_class *everywhere*; this seems like a pretty broadly-applicable use case since it is still useful even if you want to use json most of the time.

(See https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/521)

@tiangolo I noticed your git commit message "Fix mypy error in Travis not shown locally..." -- this is due to the recent update to mypy. If you run `pip install mypy --upgrade` you'll get the same output locally. (Just had to make that change on another PR myself 😄 .)

Thanks @dmontagu ! :sweat_smile:

I did, and I got the other errors you were having, in fact, I think I read your mind and implemented exactly the same fixes here at the same time as you :joy: 

But now I'm getting a really strange `mypy` error in Travis, reported in a line that doesn't even have the reported variable...

I'll rebase this from master to take your last PR and hopefully, that will fix it :rocket:

Thanks @toppk ! :cake: 

I like the idea. In fact, I've been thinking about it for quite some time. :sweat_smile: 

I refactored it a bit to move the `default_response_class` from the `APIRouter` class to the `include_router` method, the same way we do with `dependencies`. And updated the logic with the `None` checks and assignments.

This fixes a future bug where a sub router would not behave as expected.

This also allows adding the same router more than once, with different response classes. :tada: 

I also added lots of tests, to check all the possible combinations of router trees I could imagine.
review_comments:
~I think you'll be able to fix the mypy issue by using~
```
response_class = route.response_class or route.default_response_class
media_type = response_class.media_type
```

~Or by setting~
```python
self.response_class: Type[Response] = response_class or default_response_class
```
~in `APIRoute.__init__`.~
Oh nevermind, I see the problem, media_type itself could be None

Thanks! I don't know I wasn't getting the same line locally, nor the error :thinking: 

I added an assertion that requires the `route.response_class` to exist. As these utils should only be called after adding the sub-routers to the app. So, by that point, they all should be some class (and not `None`).

title: Add support for custom route class
body:
This change would make it substantially easier to override the default route-matching behavior. In particular, this could simplify the implementation of version-based routing as discussed in https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/200

I created a pull request for this to aid in discussion since it was only a three line change, but before merging it would probably merit some updates to docs and tests.

In the absence of interest, I'll be happy to just close the pull request unmerged.

title: Ensure `skip_defaults` doesn't cause extra fields to be serialized
body:
Currently, if `skip_defaults` is true, the secure cloned field is not used when serializing the response. This can lead to extra information leaking out if the `response_model` differs in type from the returned model.

This pull request fixes this bug, and updates the relevant unit test to check for it.

(The bug was introduced in #422 -- sorry about that!)

-----

I believe this pull request also improves performance in the case where `skip_defaults` is `True`, as the response will now only be validated once in the `serialize_response` call, instead of twice.

body:
Hi @tiangolo,

This `PR` fix a failure on exception system (missing `requests` as **requirements**)

Related-to: https://github.com/the-benchmarker/web-frameworks/pull/1740

Regards,

title: Make it possible to add swagger ui's clientId and appName
body:
Added 2 new parameters to FastAPI init:
- swagger_ui_client_id
- swagger_ui_app_name

Swagger UI has a method initOAuth. We are using to of these: 
- clientId
- appName

[Reference to Swagger UI OAuth2 configuration](https://swagger.io/docs/open-source-tools/swagger-ui/usage/oauth2/)

see #237

title: Disambiguation
body:
I was working on an implicit flow writing something like
```
oauth2_scheme = OAuth2(
    flows=OAuthFlows(
        implicit=OAuthFlowImplicit(
            authorizationUrl=blablabla
        )
    )
)
```
then I realized I had imported the wrong OAuth2, the one from fastapi.openapi.models while the correct one should have been from fastapi.security.oauth2

this PR, while non-essential, helps to disambiguate what I found to be a "bad" user-experience, in the meantime I have no bad feelings should it be rejected since now I know which OAuth2 I need to import :grimacing:
comments:

> **Legend** - [Click here to learn more](https://docs.codecov.io/docs/codecov-delta)

> Powered by [Codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/505?src=pr&el=footer). Last update [580cf8f...96d6ce1](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/505?src=pr&el=lastupdated). Read the [comment docs](https://docs.codecov.io/docs/pull-request-comments).

@euri10 I personally find the following versions of the names more natural (this is the way I would say it if describing it naturally):

* `OAuthFlowModelImplicit` -> `OAuthImplicitFlowModel`
* `OAuthFlowModelPassword` -> `OAuthPasswordFlowModel`
* `OAuthFlowModelClientCredentials` -> `OAuthClientCredentialsFlowModel`
* `OAuthFlowModelAuthorizationCode` -> `OAuthAuthorizationCodeFlowModel`

I recognize it removes the consistency of always starting with `OAuthFlowModel`, but I personally think it's worth it given how much more natural it is to read.

With the above name changes (well, even without them), I would definitely be in favor of merging this PR if possible -- I think putting `Model` helps convey that there is nothing special happening with these objects.

(I found it a little confusing when I started working with fastapi; I didn't realize these `OAuth` objects were really just data containers and not somehow playing some special role elsewhere within the framework.)

> @euri10 I personally find the following versions of the names more natural (this is the way I would say it if describing it naturally):

done, this is indeed much better, I stupidly used Pycharm proposed changes after I did without thinking OAuthFlow to OAuthFlowModel :)

@euri10 I was actually proposing to put the word `Flow` *after* the name of the flow. The version you just pushed is still an improvement in my opinion, but I *would* prefer it be `OAuthPasswordFlowModel` over `OAuthFlowPasswordModel`.

> @euri10 I was actually proposing to put the word `Flow` _after_ the name of the flow. The version you just pushed is still an improvement in my opinion, but I _would_ prefer it be `OAuthPasswordFlowModel` over `OAuthFlowPasswordModel`.

mm it's late and I misread you.

this reminds more of that quote:

> There are only two hard things in Computer Science: cache invalidation and naming things.
> 
> -- Phil Karlton

:laughing: 

so options are
@euri10 
OAuthFlowPassword > OAuthFlowPasswordModel
or @dmontagu 
OAuthFlowPassword > OAuthPasswordFlowModel

I think I prefer mine since it's closer to the original and it's a model for an oauth flow that deals with passwords,

but I have absolutely 0 problem changing to yours, will leave it at that for the moment, @tiangolo, ruler of the FastAPI universe will have the hard task to name things :boom: :collision: :exclamation: :question:

Thanks @euri10 ! Here's the thing, all those models are taken and named almost literally as from the OpenAPI spec.

E.g. the `OAuthFlow` class is named after the "OAuth Flow": https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/3.0.2.md#oauthFlowObject

And `OAuthFlows` after "OAuth Flows": https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/3.0.2.md#oauthFlowsObject

Having those names would also mean that a JSON Schema for OpenAPI itself could be generated from the pure models, and the automatic names would be almost identical to the spec.

How did you end up importing them? Is it an auto-import feature? Do you think there's any other way we could solve the user experience for this?

I agree that the same name in both places is unfortunate, but I also wanted to keep the names as natural and "intuitive" as possible...

Or is there any other way we can "hide" the models from OpenAPI to avoid the bad user experience?

> How did you end up importing them? Is it an auto-import feature? Do you think there's any other way we could solve the user experience for this?

I got the logic now, makes sense, as I said non-essential at all.

I pressed alt+enter in pycharm that gives hints as to what to import, and I didn't pay enough attention and imported the model

I have no idea if you can hide something from the api from the end-user perspective

title: Remove reparse
body:
This is a work in progress attempt to remove the use of the secure cloned field.

The remaining issues relate to:
1. Generic field types parameterized by pydantic models (e.g., `List[MyModel]`)
1. Some outstanding issues with pydantic dataclasses

I'm opening this despite the fact that it currently fails to share the implementation idea (and maybe get some feedback).
comments: I think this is more appropriate as a pydantic feature request combined with a smaller change to fastapi; https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/issues/811 is the corresponding pydantic feature request. I'll create a new pull request to FastAPI once that pydantic issue is addressed.

title: Make adding openapi route configurable
body:
This is an example of a [Starlette application](https://gist.github.com/alimcmaster1/c5146abad6a0b1f60ea009b589ec309d)  - the following change makes it possible for users to use a Starlette schema endpoint as a source for the docs.

Making it simple to migrate from Starlette to FastAPI:

`app = Starlette()`

`app = FastAPI(add_openapi_route=False, openapi_url='/schema')`

cc. @tiangolo  to review.

title: Preserve route_class when calling include_router
body:
Ensures that the `route_class` of the subrouter is preserved when including a subrouter in a parent router.

------

Right now, when including a router with a custom `route_class`, the `route_class` is not automatically preserved. This essentially prevents the use of custom `route_class` without special tricks. (For example, see #521, where overriding the `route_class` is used to enable msgpack parsing.)

This PR changes the behavior of `include_router` so that the route class is preserved during inclusion for any routes that are subclasses of `APIRoute`.

-----

Closes #539

@tiangolo I just re-ran the checks for this PR. Note #568 may serve as an alternative, but would require a little more effort. I'm not sure which way (if either) you'd be inclined to go.

Thanks @dmontagu ! Great! :rocket: :tada: 

I just added a couple of tests to make sure we avoid possible future regressions with this. :white_check_mark:
Thanks, sorry the PR wasn't more complete!

No worries at all, you're doing an awesome job helping with FastAPI here. With code and also helping others in issues and in Gitter.

You rock! :guitar: :rocket: :tada: :bowing_man:

Hi, I'm wondering on whether it is possible to externally override the `route_class` of an included router. A use case would be to add a logging implementation (via subclassing of `APIRoute`) to existing routers. This is currently possible only if the source is available, and only by doing it before adding the routes, but it appears not to be possible for external modules. The `route_class_override` parameter is also only available at the actual call of `add_api_route`.

The crux of the issue, I think, is that routes are eagerly constructed with the `route_class` constructor in the `add_api_route` method, and then added to the `self.routes` attribute. Hence, overriding the route_class after the fact would perhaps require to reconstruct all the routes... I'm not sure that would be desireable nor possible... 

Still, I think the use case for 'dynamic route_class overriding' is there...

Please let me know if you think this makes sense for the project, and in that case whether you would like me to submit a more refined issue.

Thank you for this great project!

title: fix bug with 4xx check
body:
The status_code_key is set to uppercase above, leading to this check always being false.
This invalidated the expected behaviour of a specified 4xx or 4XX overriding the default 422 response

title: Alphabetically sort schemas
body:
Modify openapi spec generation to include schemas in alphabetical order.

-----

Right now, the schemas occur in a haphazard order in the generated OpenAPI spec (and therefore also at the bottom of the swagger docs), since it is generated based on the order in which schemas are encountered during the spec generation process.

This change would cause the definitions to occur in alphabetical order.

Thanks @dmontagu ! :cake: :rocket: 

I haven't needed it, but I see how it could be useful. Thanks! :tada:

I have found this useful for two things:

1. In the swagger docs, sometimes I just want to scroll down to the schema definition. It's almost impossible to find the right one when they are not alphabetically sorted! (Well, maybe I just have a lot of models, but it has been a frequent nuisance.)

2. I want my schema diffs to be as small as possible (so I can put them in version control and have it be useful for tracking changes). There are workarounds (like sorting the json keys), but they mostly have tradeoffs I don't like (the resulting schema is in a very unorthodox order).

Thanks for merging this!

title: Allow api docs to be truncated and used for other purposes
body:
This is essentually the same Click does:
https://click.palletsprojects.com/en/7.x/documentation/#truncating-help-texts

It allows splitting the documenting text so that the lower part may be
used to describe the function further, e.g. extracting information using
Sphinx.
comments: This seems like a nice, simple addition. Using `"\f"` as the splitting pattern might not have been my first choice (or at least, not the first choice I considered), but given other well-established tools use it for this purpose it seems reasonable enough to me. I can't imagine it getting in the way.

I simplified the splitting, thanks for pointing it out.
If we used other characters than ``\f``, we need to test commonly used tools for their compatibility, that is, if they just ignore it. Sphinx is known to work with it just fine.
Oh also, the formatting is wrong (in at least one of the test files) -- run `./scripts/format-imports.sh` -- it should automatically fix it for you.
Okay, done. Sorry for the noise. I didn't know you could define functions without a body, but the test suite confirms it. I lean something new every day :)

The reason it works is because it has a docstring - so technically it does have a body :). At least, it has enough of one for python to not raise a syntax error. If you drop the docstring it will error.

Thank you for your contribution @svalouch ! :tada: :rocket: :cake: 

I updated it a bit and moved the docs to the section on advanced path operation configurations.

Thanks @dmontagu for the help and comments! :bowing_man:
review_comments: No need to check for `"\f" in self.description`; the first result of `split` will just be the whole string in that case

I think the build will pass if you remove this return statement, and the one from the other endpoint above.

(FastAPI has a 100% coverage requirement for the *test code* as well.)

title: Refactor APIRoute for easier overrides
body:
This pull request refactors the `APIRoute` class for two main purposes:

1. It makes it easier to override individual pieces in subclasses (e.g., `get_app`), and
2. It makes it so that `APIRouter.include_router` does not require re-instantiating `APIRoute`s, so that subclasses can be preserved.

This pull request would eliminate the need for #538 and would help address #521 and #558.
comments: (To be more explicit, this pull request also modifies the `APIRouter.include_router` so that it doesn't re-instantiate `APIRoute` instances.)

Hmm.. It doesn't like the deepcopy for python 3.6.

I'm going to wait for positive feedback on this approach before I try to put in additional work to make it work with 3.6.

title: Add support for contextmanager-style dependencies
body:
This pull request adds support for contextmanager style dependencies. 

In particular, dependencies of the following form would be supported:

```python
def get_dependency():
    value = do_setup()
    yield value  # the value received by the endpoint
    do_cleanup()  # handled in a background task after returning the response
```

Async contextmanager dependencies are also supported. (See the tests for examples of each, along with a demonstration of error handling.)

I've opened the pull request as a draft because I still need to add tutorial examples and/or other docs, but I am not currently aware of any remaining issues in the implementation.

(Please let me know if you see any.)

Note this would make it possible to stop using middleware for the ubiquitous `get_db` dependency:
```python
@app.middleware("http")
async def db_session_middleware(request: Request, call_next):
    response = Response("Internal server error", status_code=500)
    try:
        request.state.db = SessionLocal()
        response = await call_next(request)
    finally:
        request.state.db.close()
    return response


# Dependency
def get_db(request: Request):
    return request.state.db
```
could become
```python
def get_db() -> Session:
    db = SessionLocal()
    try:
        yield db
    finally:
        db.close()
```

In particular, this would let you drop the database dependency on routes that don't need it. (In my experience, middleware like this is one of the things that makes it painful to reuse code across projects.)

>         raise exc
>     finally:

@tiangolo This now has a first "serious" attempt at adding docs for this feature. Let me know if you have any preferences for changes to their structure.

This looks interesting, although I can't follow quite how the code actually works, likely due to my shallow knowledge of async stuff. Some questions:

- How does this interact with the caching of resolved depedencies?

- How do I make sure the order of teardown is correct? For example, if I have a transaction as a dependency, and that transaction must be finished before the database connection is torn down, how do I express that requirement?

- How is error handling supported? A common pattern for me is semantically expressed like this:

   ```
   conn = connect_to_db()
   t = conn.transaction()
   try:
        ... do work ...
       t.commit()
   except:
       t.rollback()
    conn.close()
   ```

If `conn` and `t` were two context manager dependencies, how could I ensure the behaviour as a above?

Thanks @dmontagu ! This is pretty smart! :clap: :rocket: 

I got inspired by this and started to check different cases. Sadly, if an exception occurs inside of a path operation or a dependency that requires the context dependency, the `except` and `finally` blocks of the context dependency are not executed. So, it ends up not behaving like a context manager with respect to handling internal exceptions.

---

But then I realized that we can achieve it in a different way, I'm currently sketching a PR for it.

I needed to have the most recent interface of Starlette (ASGI 3), so I merged that first. But with that I'm pretty sure that we'll be able to support it.

The only drawback is that I'll have to add 2 dependencies (backports) to support Python 3.6:

https://github.com/python-trio/async_generator and https://github.com/sorcio/async_exit_stack

The other option would be to only support context manager dependencies in Python 3.7.

@tiangolo Good catch. The BackgroundTask approach definitely felt a little bit fragile, so I'm not surprised that there were some edge cases not being handled properly.

I think it would make sense to only support this with python 3.7. I also don't see much downside to adding optional dependencies to support it with python 3.6. But I use 3.7 everywhere now (and will hopefully be able to change that to 3.8 ASAP) so I'm fine either way.

-----

@cjw296 As implemented, it would require some care to ensure teardown was done in a guaranteed order. I think it would currently amount to constructing a consistent "teardown" dependency. But I think in most cases, you would be able to just build a single dependency that sets everything up in order, and tears it down in order, and then to build other dependencies on top of that. For example, handling your use case:

```python
def db_contextmanager():
    conn = connect_to_db()
    t = conn.transaction()
    try:
         yield conn, t
        t.commit()
    except:
        t.rollback()
     conn.close()

def get_connection(conn_t = Depends(db_contextmanager)):
    conn, t = conn_t
    return conn

def get_transaction(conn_t = Depends(db_contextmanager)):
    conn, t = conn_t
    return t

def endpoint(conn = Depends(get_connection)):
    # Do something with the connection
    pass
```

I think there is room for improvement here though, as this design isn't very composable.

The cleanest logic I've been able to think of for how to have contextmanager dependencies tear themselves down in a controllable order would be to merge the individual teardown tasks into a single "solved" teardown task by basically walking the reversed dependency DAG (restricted to contextmanager dependencies).

The idea being that if you have contextmanager dependencies d1 and d2, and d2 depends on d1, then the cleanup section of d2 would be guaranteed to execute prior to the cleanup section of d1. That maybe doesn't cover all theoretical use cases, but I think it would cover 99+% of practical uses and you could always work around the other cases by just writing a more complex contextmanager dependency.
Closing in favor of #595
review_comments: Note: this is precisely the same error type and message you'd get if `call` had been used in a `with` statement after decorating with `contextlib.contextmanager`.
The same is the case for the `RuntimeError` above and those in the `solve_asyncgen` call below.

title: Add better support for request body access/manipulation
body:
This pull request introduces a small change to `APIRoute` to make it easier to construct a custom `app` attribute in a custom subclass.

There is a lot of custom functionality that this change would simplify, but there are two especially important capabilities it adds:
* A way to get handle exceptions with access to the request body
* A way to modify the request body prior to its parsing/handling by FastAPI

I've added two sections to the "Using the Request Directly" docs showing how to use the changes in this PR to implement both of the above kinds of feature.

------

There have been many recent github issues that could be better-addressed with this capability.

**Related to accessing the body in middleware/exception handlers:**
* #180 accessing request body in middleware
* #375 accessing request body in exception handlers
* #394 accessing request body in middleware
* #575 accessing request body in exception handlers
* https://github.com/encode/starlette/issues/493 accessing request body in exception handlers (using fastapi)
* https://github.com/encode/starlette/issues/495  a variety of use cases involving accessing request body in middleware (some using fastapi)

**Related to modifying the request prior to handling by FastAPI:**
* #521 support for msgpack-encoded requests
* https://github.com/encode/starlette/issues/644 support for gzip-encoded requests (using fastapi)

-----
Quoting Tom Christie from https://github.com/encode/starlette/issues/495:

> "Consume request body in middleware is problematic"
>
> Indeed. Consuming request data in middleware is problematic.
> Not just to Starlette, but generally, everywhere.
>
> On the whole you should avoid doing so if at all possible.

I think using middleware to consume the request body is the obvious (or, perhaps, "naive") way to handle these issues. But that approach is explicitly discouraged, and not supported. So I think the approach described in / supported by this PR is especially valuable.

-----

(This pull request is in lieu of https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/568, and includes what I think is both the smallest and most important change to `APIRoute` from that pull request.)

I think this is sufficiently important to have its own section in the docs, so I moved it. I put it near the end as I consider this quite advanced and it could confuse newcomers.

review_comments:
Not sure if I'm describing things correctly in this block 😅, just wanted to flag that.

Also I'm not sure if the new `APIRoute.get_app` should be renamed. Since it just calls the `get_app` function that seemed like a good name to me though.

Good point!

The name `get_app` is probably confusing for others.

The original name was based on how it was used in Starlette, which in turn was based on the ASGI spec (technically, a request handler is an ASGI app itself, that could stand by itself).

---

But it could clearly be confusing. I think we should rename it.

How about something like:

*  `request_function`
* `get_request_function`
* `request_handler`
* `get_request_handler`
* `get_handler`

What do you think?

Also, I think we should name the stand-alone function one way and the class method something different, to avoid the editor linking the function from inside the method to the method itself (instead of the function that lives outside).
I'm in favor of `get_request_handler` for the function and `get_route_handler` for the method. (As discussed on gitter.)

title: Dependencies with yield (used as context managers)
body:
Add support for context manager dependencies using functions with `yield`. Sync or async.

With inspiration from @dmontagu 's https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/570.

This handles dependencies that `yield` by converting them into a sync or async context manager,  depending on the function. And runs them in a "context manager stack".

This way, the order of dependencies is preserved and cleanup/closing happens in the correct order (the first dependency called is cleaned last).

This also means that dependencies that require other dependencies will still have them on scope during cleanup. So, contextmanager-style dependencies that require other contextmanager dependencies will have access to them during cleanup.

When a dependency function is a normal function (not `async`), the context manager is called in a threadpool (`__enter__`), so, the same as with normal dependencies, it's safe to do non-async blocking IO in contextmanager style dependencies. E.g. start a DB connection, probably the main use-case for this. And the same for cleaning it up (`__exit__`).

This works by default in Python 3.7.

And works in Python 3.6 if the backports are installed:

* `async-exit-stack`
* `async-generator`

```bash
pip install async-exit-stack async-generator
```

But they are not forced/required dependencies. If an app doesn't use context managers and still runs in Python 3.6, it will still work without the backports.

...Although I suspect most of the apps will use this for DB connections.

These backport dependencies are checked on app initialization, so, if a contextmanager-style dependency function (with `yield`s) is found, the environment is not Python 3.7+, and the backports are not installed, it will raise a `RuntimeError` right away on app initialization (so, no risk for the error lurking into a prod environment).

---

TODO:

* [x] Fix `mypy` types.
* [x] Extend tests to check for correct order of contextmanager dependencies with subdependencies.
* [x] Extend tests to verify that background tasks can still access the dependencies from context managers (e.g. DB connections).
* [x] Add docs.
comments: This is looking really great!

```suggestion
A "middleware" is basically a function that is always executed for each request, with some code executed before, and some code executed after the endpoint function.
```

```suggestion
FastAPI supports dependencies that do some <abbr title='sometimes also called "exit", "cleanup", "teardown", "close", "context managers", ...'>extra steps after finishing</abbr>.
```

The preference of `pip install`, `python -m pip install`, `python3 -m pip install`, `pip3 install`, is quite subjective and varies a lot depending on personal taste.

title: Prevent adding HTTP 422 to the responses when all params are optional
body:
Heya 👋,

After reading https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/497 I thought I'd give it a try. This PR will prevent HTTP 422 responses from being added to the operation when it only has optional arguments. It should fix the bug that occurs in the code of #497 : 

```
from fastapi.applications import FastAPI
from fastapi import APIRouter
app = FastAPI(docs_url="/")
router = APIRouter()


@router.get('/router')
async def handler(code: str = ''):
    print(code)
    return "hello world"


app.include_router(router)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    import uvicorn
    uvicorn.run(app, port=8001, reload=True)
```

### How to test:
1. Setup environment with the example code above
1. Acknowledge that the swagger ui shows a HTTP 422 as possible response code
1. Checkout PR
1. Acknowledge that the swagger ui no longer shows HTTP 422 as a possible response code.
1. Change your route params to have a required route
1. Acknowledge that the HTTP 422 is now back 🎉

title: Fix #259: generate OpenAPI docs for responses with no content.
body:
This is a backwards-compatible change, as far as I know, for responses that explicitly return content.  There are two basic changes:

* FastAPI now knows which response codes do not return bodies, and suppresses the `content` element in their API docs.
* If the response_class for a route has no media type, FastAPI assumes that it will not return content.  (If this route specifies additional responses that do return content, those responses are assumed to return `application/json` content and use an appropriate response class.)

This change introduces two new test files which check the new logic and keep the code coverage at 100%.

This change also updates the tutorial documentation to reflect the new functionality.
comments: FWIW, I ran the black reformatter on all the newly contributed code, to make sure it's stylistically correct.

title: Fixing validator-caused incorrect output key order
body:
A quick PR that fixes #630. The cloned field was adding existing field validators before the fields themselves. 

This caused Python's default "ordered `dict`" behaviour to incorrectly output dictionary key/value pairs with all 'validated' fields first.
comments:
Have you confirmed that this doesn't change the validation logic? Probably worth making sure validators still work on endpoints with a response model (maybe this is already tested in the existing tests).

Pydantic has some metaprogramming magic that could mean the validators don't get set up properly if they aren't present during class initialization; I'm not sure.

@tiangolo I recognize this is a lot of changes in a single PR, and may be painful to work through (mostly minor changes, but still). Let me know if there is anything I can do to make it easier for you to review.

Also, more generally, let me know whether you think this PR has a chance of being merged (if you want to do things in a fundamentally different way or just want to implement it yourself, I'll close this). If you are potentially interested in merging this PR though, I'll start work on updating the docs too.

Also, @tiangolo, not sure when you think you'll be able to get to this, but it would be really great to quickly update the supported pydantic version once https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/pull/722 is merged (the mypy plugin for pydantic).

@samuelcolvin said he'd be able to take a closer look at that PR some time this upcoming week; if the response is positive and it is likely to be merged in the short term, it would be great if we could update directly to supporting that version of pydantic, even if it meant waiting a little longer to get this merged. (Or at least get a second quick release if necessary when the update to pydantic is released.)

------

Alternatively, maybe FastAPI can move to a looser pinning of pydantic (e.g., `"^1.0"`) now that it has hit a major version? That would solve the issue completely, *and* make it easier to incorporate any additional new pydantic features!
I'll look at the mypy plugin soon, is it blocking the fastapi upgrade? Hopefully it'll be added as pydantic v1.1.

No, it doesn't block the fastapi upgrade at all, I would just really like to use it, and would be sad if the v1 upgrade happened quickly but then it took a while to get compatibility with v1.1 (or whenever it gets merged).

It's not a big deal either way (I can always just continue to copy and paste the mypy extension into each of my projects), it's just a pain because I have many projects all with slightly different versions of it as I have made minor fixes over time.
Great. Like you say, pydantic should strictly follow semvar now, so fastapi should work with all version 1 releases.
+1 for this PR, very much looking forward to using the new features of pydantic in my project

Also :+1: for this PR, I'm planning to build a new API project using FastAPI, but the upcoming breaking change results in some concerns and I might have trouble pitching the choice of FastAPI until there is some clarity.
@dmontagu can I help with pushing this PR through?

Thanks @dmontagu ! Awesome job! :clap: :clap: :clap: :cake: :rocket:

And thanks for the comments in the PR, they help a lot! :nerd_face: :memo: :1st_place_medal: 

---

I added some code to make it backwards compatible with Pydantic 0.32.2 with a lot of deprecation warnings. That should help developers migrating code bases.

Now all the tests still pass with Pydantic 0.32.2 except the tests for the updated OpenAPI schemas, as the schema titles now have spaces instead of underscores, and the strict booleans. But all the rest still passes. That means that developer's codebases that somehow require Pydantic 0.32.2 will still work as expected in the next version of FastAPI, although with deprecation warnings. 

I didn't add extra tests for that, and all the backwards compatible checks in FastAPI's codebase look kinda ugly and cumbersome, but I bet that might help some developers doing the migration more smoothly. Later, in a future version, I'll remove all that.

I tried extending the range to Pydantic v1.1.1, but as in FastAPI we are using some of the internals of Pydantic (things that wouldn't be covered by SemVer, so, it's not Pydantic's fault), some things have changed and break stuff here, so I think I'll just have to keep it pinned and update it often when there are new releases of Pydantic.

We can debug and fix what changed in v1.1.1 later, but that'll be in another PR, this one is good as it is right now.

I also updated the docs for the examples that changed.

---

This is ready to be merged now, I think everything's fine. And I see you dobule-triple-checked everything too :nerd_face: :100:

But as I added some extra stuff, and this is a big refactor, I wanted to give you the chance to check if you see anything that doesn't seem right, although it's not necessary. Otherwise, I'll just merge it tomorrow and release :rocket:
review_comments: Looks like `iterate_in_threadpool` was unused -- I'm guessing maybe this was a carryover from work on the contextmanager dependencies. I removed it, but let me know if you meant to keep it for some reason.
Need to pass the param name to this function now for the purpose of error messages (it's a new requirement of the pydantic function it calls).
This is where `param_name` is now used, referenced above.

It looked like in each of the `if` branch conditions, you were looking up the attribute on the field; that seemed unnecessary (I don't think any of the functions called could change it), so I just kept the value from the first cast call and used it afterward.
config argument is no longer necessary

Actually, I was planning on importing everything from Starlette, and making it available from FastAPI, so that users (and even myself) don't have to guess what to import from FastAPI and what from Starlette.

And as I was creating that "context manager in threadpool" that was not provided by Starlette, I thought of including `iterate_in_threadpool` right away...

Anyway, that's the reason. But I'll finish that in a PR later. :sweat_smile:
Perfect, thanks for the clarification.
Cool :+1: thanks.
Great, thanks.

Agreed! It makes sense to keep in line with Pydantic.

I added some backward compatibility with warnings :nerd_face:
Perfect. This also helped while to add the backward compatibility parts :sweat_smile: :rocket:
Cool! I think it's fine :+1: :rocket:
Perfect, thanks for the explanation. :rocket:
Nice! :sparkles:

title: Fix #679: allow async class methods as dependencies
body:
This is the fix for issue #679: allow to use async class methods as dependencies.

Before, such methods were not correctly detected as coroutine and thus were not awaited but rather sent to `run_in_threadpool`. We ended up then injecting the coroutine instead of its result.

I've used [`inspect.isroutine`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/inspect.html#inspect.isroutine), instead of doing `inspect.isfunction(call) or inspect.ismethod(call)`. It seems to fulfill our need while saving us a check.

I also took this opportunity to add some unit tests to verify the different use cases regarding class dependencies (callable, async callable, sync methods and async methods).

It could also be interesting to mention those possibilities in the [Classes as Dependencies](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/dependencies/classes-as-dependencies/) section of the documentation. What do you think?

title: Clarify docs for APIRouter dependencies
body:
Someone might expect that the dependencies specified on an APIRouter be passed along to their path operation functions, though the behavior is closer to dependencies specified in path operation decorators. Being explicit here will make the behavior unambiguous.

This is especially true for someone who might be jumping around in the documentation and who wouldn't necessarily have read [dependencies in path operation decorators](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/dependencies/dependencies-in-path-operation-decorators/).
comments:
Great, thanks @marier-nico ! :memo: :cake: :tada: 

Thanks for the explanation / argument, and thanks for writing the update to the docs using the same conventions as in the rest of the docs (with italics for those keywords/names), very perceptive! :nerd_face:
No problem, I'm happy I could contribute to the project! :smile:

title: Adding support for documenting OpenAPI callbacks through APIRoutes
body:
Added support for automatic generation and validation (via pydantic) of OpenAPI callback objects.

This is currently done by providing a list of `APIRoute` objects to a new keyword argument in the path decorators (post, get, put, etc.).

This is convenient, as that list can easily be compiled by creating an `APIRouter` object, and defining the expected (callback) routes. This has the added benefit that all Request and Response Schemas are added to the documentation, and the router used for defining the callbacks makes a solid base for mocking/testing the callback endpoints that a client would implement.

Please let me know if you have any comments/concerns.
Right now, the tests are failing because the coverage decreased a little. I'll try to see if this can be fixed. I can also squash and rebase my changes if needed.

Fixes issue #673
Thanks! I'll review it soon.
@tiangolo  - any update on this? Anything you'd like to discuss?

@dmontagu - thanks for taking the time to review the changes! I'll incorporate the changes you requested, and rebase the code against the latest release.

Before moving ahead though, I just want to confirm that you are OK with the approach of defining the callbacks in this manner, and that I'm not abusing any of the existing constructs 😉

@booooh 

It looks like you are properly following all conventions, as far as I can tell. 👍

I haven't closely read the openapi spec for callbacks yet, and probably would want to before merging this, but as long as the schema you are outputting matches the OpenAPI spec I would be happy to support this feature, and I think the implementation here is good.

-----

At some point I would like to refactor the endpoint creation process so that we don't have to copy the long list of openapi keyword arguments into each of the different route decorator methods; it makes adding features like this somewhat daunting / painful. But approaches to that have been brought up in other issues, and for now I am happy to continue following that convention.

Yep, this looks good, thanks for making this! :rocket: :cake: 

Maybe it feels weird to have a full router to declare endpoints that will never be used, but most of the logic was already there, and it will be the most familiar thing to use for FastAPI users, so I think it's fine, it's the most sensible approach.

For the rest, agreed with @dmontagu (thanks for the review!).

I also want to check some things that could be updated later, but some I don't know if they would work or not, I'll do it afterwards on top of your changes.
I'm currently working on this, I'll send my updates on top of your branch once they are done. So you can just wait a bit.

Thanks! :tada: :rocket: 

I updated it a bit. I also added some extras that improve how this feature works.

Some of the updates:

* Remove unnecessary code, logs.
* Update type declarations.
* Update `operationId` generation, to ensure all of them are valid Python names.
* Update tests according to the `operationId`s that changed.
* Include a `default_response_class` parameter when creating an `APIRouter`, to simplify the creation of these callbacks.
* Update/increase docs and tutorial example.
review_comments:
```suggestion
There are cases where the API that is being developed will require the clients to implement callback handlers that will in turn be invoked
```
```suggestion Since this is a common usecase, the OpenAPI spec defines how these callbacks should be documented: OpenAPI callback object spec, ```
```suggestion and there are also some more detailed examples available: Swagger callback example. ```

```suggestion
Clients of a `FastAPI` application may need to register callback handlers that will be notified when certain events occur.
```

You should rebase against the most recent release; I think some of the minor formatting changes (like the extra commas) will go away. Also, don’t worry about bumping the version, @tiangolo will take care of that before the next release.
Thanks! Yep, it's not needed.

Applies `jsonable_encoder` to pydantic error output in case the context is not JSON serializable.

Closes https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/743

title: Enable full Travis for Python 3.8
body:
:construction_worker: Enable full Travis for Python 3.8, not only Python 3.8 dev. And remove flag allowing it to fail.

I can do it now that [Uvloop has a version that supports Python 3.8](https://github.com/MagicStack/uvloop/releases/tag/v0.14.0), so Uvicorn supports 3.8, and Starlette supports 3.8. And Pydantic is already testing and supporting 3.8 as well.

LGTM

I think this needs some performance checks first. `jsonable_encoder` is already a bottleneck in many cases. For a long list of a type far down the list I could see this PR increasing the encoding time by a large factor.

For comparison, does anyone know how json.dumps handles this (with a custom encoders)?

review_comments:
Should this be here? It looks like you'll add the exception to the errors list even if the next block succeeds?

I think the errors list should remain empty if data conversion works?

Can I ask what is the rationale for using `vars` here instead of `dict`? `vars` may have subtly different behavior for custom types that override `__iter__`, so in theory this could be a difficult-to-debug and difficult-to-work-around breaking change.

I did a check, and `if custom_encoder:` is ~3-4x faster than `for encoder_type, encoder in custom_encoder.items():` when custom_encoder is empty. So I think this branch should be behind an `if custom_encoder:` check.

(Note that this function calls itself recursively for lists and dicts, so should be considered a hot-loop function, and should be implemented as performantly as possible. It currently adds **substantial** overhead to serialization for large lists and dicts.)

I retained the previous behaviour but since I am using explicit instance checking now instead of catching a keyerror all the exception catching was moved 1 indentation to the left. It is the github diff displaying that makes it looks as if I changed this.

Well as soon as one of the attempts to fill the `data` variable succeeds the function returns and this list of errors is garbage collected. This is the way the originial implementation worked and I think it's an ok implementation?

Well you said earlier: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/756#issuecomment-560042441 that since this is only for custom encoders it wasn't too important, that's why I didn't bother.

I can definitely do some performance testing and speed it up significantly with your suggestion though!

The thing that concerns me is that there are some relatively commonly used types in here:
https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/blob/31cac4783b2cba2836e7bf2b4a593a3085930da6/pydantic/json.py#L20

At the very least, `Decimal` is somewhat common, and it is at the end of the list. Either way, it's probably worth a PR to pydantic to reorder based on most common usage. But that can be separate.

title: Update gendered language to remove male bias
body: Changed 'he' to the singular 'they' in handling exceptions section of tutorial

> The diff coverage is `n/a`.

[![Impacted file tree graph](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/761/graphs/tree.svg?width=650&token=llDQVnTfnB&height=150&src=pr)](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/761?src=pr&el=tree)

[Continue to review full report at Codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/761?src=pr&el=continue).
> **Legend** - [Click here to learn more](https://docs.codecov.io/docs/codecov-delta)
> `Δ = absolute <relative> (impact)`, `ø = not affected`, `? = missing data`
> Powered by [Codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/761?src=pr&el=footer). Last update [c200bc2...c10dfe3](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/761?src=pr&el=lastupdated). Read the [comment docs](https://docs.codecov.io/docs/pull-request-comments).

Thanks for your contribution. :memo: :woman: :man: :cake: 

I think readability and understandability are very important, and "they" might seem a bit ambiguous.

The word should actually be "it", but as "it" could be interpreted as referring to the "error" instead of the "client", I initially used "he", to make it unambiguous.

I updated it in #780 to remove the he/it/they and make it more explicit while keeping understandability. So I'll close this now.
review_comments: Typo. The should be They

title: Make OAuth2PasswordRequestFormStrict importable from fastapi.security package
body: Unfortunately, everything from `fastapi.security.oauth2` available from `fastapi.security` package, except `OAuth2PasswordRequestFormStrict`. I've added the missing entry.

But that's in fact intentional.

It's to prevent someone from accidentally using it just because it's easy to import, while most of the people should be using `OAuth2PasswordRequestForm` instead.

title: Refactor logging
body:
:loud_sound: Refactor logging.

* Use the same logger everywhere to make sure the custom `fastapi` logger is used.
* Update logging strings to fix format.
* Update log level of `email-validator` errors to `INFO`, not `WARNING`.

title: Add docs for correctly using Peewee
body:
:memo: Add docs for correctly using Peewee ORM with FastAPI.

Handling all the complex threads details that can generate difficult to replicate, apparently random errors, that would most probably only show up during a somewhat heavy load with concurrent clients.

title: Implement OAuth2 authorization_code integration
body:
FastAPI currently implements the password flow grant. While this is sufficient for building applications that self-host identity, it is not the recommended OAuth2 implementation. It is also not preferred when attempting to use external identity providers. This PR would allow three-legged OAuth to be utilized by FastAPI with the OpenAPI integration.

This is a working implementation: https://github.com/kuwv/python-microservices
https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-oauth-security-topics-13#section-3.4
(edit: grammar, references)

I'm in favor of this; defer to @tiangolo though.

It will need tests and documentation, but if you wanted to wait for @tiangolo to confirm interest before building those out, that would make sense to me.

I knew coverage was going to be the issue. I commented on a previous issue. The trick is that a mock IdP would need to be setup. I planned to do a write-up discussing the example I put together to help.

I don't think you need to mock out an identity provider (and would in fact prefer you didn't! unless you add more to this PR) -- the dependency you've written just returns the authorization scheme param, so testing that it returns the right value should be enough.

I think it's enough to just *explain* how it should be used in the documentation; I don't think you need to actually implement a mocked out identity provider. (Well, that's my opinion anyway!)
Sure, I see that in the other tests. I'll give it a try next.
@tiangolo could I get your input on this? I'd like to see this implemented. If you give the go ahead I can start documenting an example implementation.
This looks good to me. Any chance you could add a brief discussion somewhere in the docs?

> This looks good to me. Any chance you could add a brief discussion somewhere in the docs?

Yeah, I was going to wait for additional feedback but I can begin it sometime later this week.

I would vote for a section in the security docs somewhere under this one: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/security/oauth2-jwt/ describing 1) (briefly) how password flow differs from authorization code flow, and 2) a basic example of how the models you've added are intended to be used.

For what it's worth, I think this would be a substantial and meaningful addition to the docs! I haven't found a great description (brief or otherwise) of the relationship between the OAuth flows, not to mention anything practical.

Once you've added this, I'd also like to integrate the authorization code flow into my (work-in-progress) fastapi-auth package (https://github.com/dmontagu/fastapi-auth.).

Sure, that would be great. I plan to do a complete working example, but I'm not sure how it will fit yet.

```suggestion
    if not (correct_username and correct_password):
```

Otherwise, if the username is bad but the password is good, the check will pass, as well as if the username is good, the check will pass regardless of the password.

In general, I would be in favor of anything that makes it easier to reusably-specify additional responses (as right now it just isn't very ergonomic), but I'm not sure I love this approach for the following reasons:

1. It seems difficult-to-discover and doesn't feel particularly consistent with the way things work elsewhere in the library
2. It does nothing to identify or warn against conflicts coming from different dependencies that could result in a response with the same error code

Point 2 would be easy enough to address, but I think point 1 will pose some issues.

------

More generally, I think it would make more sense to have a single attribute on any endpoint/dependency/etc. that would be a place that you could add a variety of additional information to inject into the OpenAPI schema. This would make the behavior consistent for a variety of potential modifications, and would make it easier to package the functionality up into a more user-friendly decorator.

I proposed something similar as a way of integrating with the starlette 0.13 routing-table-style approach to declaring routes, and I think it could make sense to support the same functionality for dependencies as a way to more consistently address #501 and other OpenAPI-related feature requests.
Defining own dunders looks very very bad for me

Thanks for the effort and proposal @Trim21 . 

And thanks, everyone, for the discussion here.

I prefer to have dependencies as normal functions. I want to support this through a new way to "register" dependencies with their additional responses in `FastAPI` and `APIRouter` instances. Hopefully, I'll be able to add that soon.

Hi @tiangolo! Was there any progress with this?

I am looking for a way to document response headers in dependency, but I haven't found anything about it except this PR.

title: bugfix bug ID #821
body:
[BUG] [DOC] None should be checked with is None in docs #821

Hi All

the library and the documentation are fantastic

I am a QA eng., and teach Python for my colleagues.
I saw in some examples that None is checked as it would be a boolean expression
for example:
https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/body/

async def create_item(item_id: int, item: Item, q: str = None):
result = {"item_id": item_id, **item.dict()}
if q:

I saw that the documentation is written for beginners too.
I think checking the None as it would be a boolean expression in the docs encourages beginners to use this formula instead of checking with is operator. And it is not good, because not None will them result unexpected results.

These examples should be corrected.

BR,
George

> Powered by [Codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/824?src=pr&el=footer). Last update [7a44540...879e348](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/824?src=pr&el=lastupdated). Read the [comment docs](https://docs.codecov.io/docs/pull-request-comments).

Admittedly, this is a question of personal preference, but for performance reasons I would advise against the use of

```python
q is not None and len(q) > 0
```

There is a relatively high variance, but it seems to run ~2-3x slower than just `if q:` for string inputs:

```python
import time

N = 1_000_000
q = "abc"

# Warm up
for _ in range(N):
    pass

t0 = time.time()
for _ in range(N):
    if q:
        pass

t1 = time.time()
print(t1 - t0)
# 0.07553

t0 = time.time()
for _ in range(N):
    if q is not None and len(q) > 0:
        pass
t1 = time.time()
print(t1 - t0)
# 0.20973
```

If you want to preserve the semantics but make the null check explicit, a more performant (and in my opinion more pythonic) alternative would be:
```python
if q is not None and q:
```
But this is still ~20% slower than just `if q:`.

In general, many linters in many languages will raise warnings for a check of the form `if len(x) > 0` instead of `if x.is_empty()` (which is typically done via just `if x` in python), due to the overhead involved with grabbing the length and then comparing it to zero. Since checks like this can frequently happen inside relatively hot loops in python, I think it is worth being in the habit of thinking about performance (as long as your code remains pythonic, which I would argue this pattern is).

------

Admittedly for an endpoint like this, the overhead of the checks is not going to be significant. But since the whole point of the PR is to help expose beginners to better practices, I'm not sure that I would say this is a strict improvement on that front.

--------

In this case though, it's not clear to me that the goal should really be to keep the semantics exactly the same -- if q="", I think it would be reasonable to perform the update.

So then it could make sense to change to `if q is not None`. I would totally agree that `if q is not None` is better than `if q` if that is a reasonable modification to the semantics.
I think `if q is not None` will be good, also pydantic which will check the string type. I will rewrite the PR accordingly

Thanks for your contribution and interest @GeorgeFischhof :memo: 

But I would prefer to keep it as is. As @dmontagu says, it's a bit more of a personal preference than best practices.

In fact, using `q is not None` would mean that `q` would be used even if it has an empty string `""`, which is probably not the intention. But thanks again for your time and interest! :rocket:

title: add body to RequestValidationError for easier tracing and debugging
body:
FastAPI is awesome, and it's schema validation is one of it's key elements. 
While debugging issues in production, the current errors data isn't sufficient to understand if it's a bad schema definition (when the API is used by multiple sources, in different ways) or really a client defect.
Adding the body data to the error can help trace problems and reproducing the issue.

> Powered by [Codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/853?src=pr&el=footer). Last update [3eca945...21aadbb](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/853?src=pr&el=lastupdated). Read the [comment docs](https://docs.codecov.io/docs/pull-request-comments).
Can this be made optional? For sensitive bodies one might not want it to be in the error, depending on how they log them.

> Can this be made optional? For sensitive bodies one might not want it to be in the error, depending on how they log them.

Handling and logging of the error hasn't changed, so it's up to the user of the framework what to do with this data.
For example in my application I wish to have this so when I catch and handle this exception, I'd be able to get the body that caused the parsing exception.

For example using sentry sdk that logs the whole exception automatically out of the box. The body will be logged and will be up to the user to reconfigure his sentry to don’t log the body part. So this might change how logging should be handled in some applications.

> For example using sentry sdk that logs the whole exception automatically out of the box. The body will be logged and will be up to the user to reconfigure his sentry to don’t log the body part. So this might change how logging should be handled in some applications.

My opinion then is it's upto the middleware to handle this case, because I can't think of a pretty way to do this in a configurable manner...
Me neither :D, just worried because this is something that caught me in the past (using sentry). I think it’s nice to have this information btw

> Me neither :D, just worried because this is something that caught me in the past (using sentry). I think it’s nice to have this information btw

Let's see what @tiangolo or other maintainer has to say then :)

Thanks for your contribution @aviramha ! :rocket: 

And thanks for the discussion and points touched @victoraugustolls .

---

So, about Sentry, the current behavior is, if you have an app like:

```Python
import sentry_sdk
from pydantic import BaseModel
from sentry_sdk import capture_exception
from sentry_sdk.integrations.asgi import SentryAsgiMiddleware
from starlette.responses import PlainTextResponse

from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.exceptions import RequestValidationError

app = FastAPI()


class User(BaseModel):
    name: str
    age: int


@app.exception_handler(RequestValidationError)
async def validation_exception_handler(request, exc: Exception):
    capture_exception(exc)
    return PlainTextResponse(str(exc), status_code=400)


@app.post("/users/")
def create_user(user: User):
    return user


sentry_sdk.init(dsn="https://deadbeef12345@sentry.io/6789")

app.add_middleware(SentryAsgiMiddleware)
```

And you post invalid data like:

```JSON
{
  "name": "string",
  "age": "a"
}
```

As that body is part of the traceback for the `RequestValidationError`, it will be sent to Sentry (I just tested it).

But it would only happen if the developer manually captures the exception for Sentry.

If the developer doesn't do it, nothing happens.

And this is all with the current implementation, before this PR.

---

The possibly sensitive data won't be sent automatically to Sentry, as by default, the exception is caught in FastAPI's code. Sensitive data would only be sent to Sentry if the developer explicitly sends the data or the exception. As @aviramha says, it would be up to the middleware how to handle it. If the developer wants to capture the exception and send it to Sentry, then that developer would have to make sure that any sensitive data is not a problem. And that still holds independently of this PR.

What I see this PR would do is allow to more easily handle and debug those bodies. In fact, it could help reducing sensitive data sent to a third party like Sentry, as it would be easier to log the failing data without having to resort to capture it and send it to Sentry or similar to be able to debug it.

---

I'll make a couple of tweaks to the PR first but I think this is a good idea :bulb: :+1: :tada: :cake:

Thanks for the elaborate comment! I'm glad you liked my idea.
Can't wait for it to get upstream.
Thanks for your contribution! :cake: :rocket:

title: Use return type annotation for response_model by default.
body:
This shouldn't be merged as-is, because I haven't added related tests yet, but I wanted to send this up for discussion. In my use case I am basically already returning the correct models from the endpoint functions already, and have return types enforced through mypy, so it feels quite unnecessary to duplicate the return type to the response_model argument to get the correct OpenAPI docs.

Basically I ended up creating a custom router that does similar logic as in this PR, but I am curious why it is not the default, since this behaviour would feel intuitive to me. I'm not offended at all if this gets rejected, but thought it's easier to explain what I'm asking through a PR instead of trying to explain it verbally in an issue.

> **Legend** - [Click here to learn more](https://docs.codecov.io/docs/codecov-delta)
> `Δ = absolute <relative> (impact)`, `ø = not affected`, `? = missing data`
> Powered by [Codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/875?src=pr&el=footer). Last update [3eca945...b181a52](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/875?src=pr&el=lastupdated). Read the [comment docs](https://docs.codecov.io/docs/pull-request-comments).

Thanks for your interest @juhovh-aiven ! :sparkles: 

Check the note here in the "Technical Details": https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/response-model/#response_model_include-and-response_model_exclude

> The response model is declared in this parameter instead of as a function return type annotation, because the path function may not actually return that response model but rather return a `dict`, database object or some other model, and then use the `response_model` to perform the field limiting and serialization.

It also means that if you return a `dict` but declare the return as a Pydantic model, mypy would rightfully complain, because you are not really returning the data type you declared your function to return.

It would be quite easy that in many cases the `response_model` doesn't really match the type of data that you are actually returning from your function, so it easily end up leading to confussion and mypy errors.

I'll close this PR, but feel free to add more comments, issues, or new PRs.

> Check the note here in the "Technical Details": https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/response-model/#response_model_include-and-response_model_exclude

Thanks for a quick response!

I had actually already checked that note in the "Technical Details", and I also use that feature quite heavily elsewhere (returning a dict and then validating/limiting fields through a pydantic model). However, my suggestion was simply about removing duplication, i.e. **if** the `response_model` and return annotation type are identical, one could skip the `response_model` part and simply go with the return annotation. If they are not identical one could still return a dict from the callable and simply set the `response_model` manually as before, the `response_model` value would always override whatever return annotation type is given.

But as mentioned, it's pretty easy to do with a custom route class with its own constructor, so I'll just keep going with that. Thank you for your good work with FastAPI, I must say I quite like it.

This was added in https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1436 :rocket: It's available in FastAPI `0.89.0`.

The new docs are here: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/response-model/ :nerd_face:

title: Fix Pydantic ORM mode blocking the event loop
body:
:bug: Fix FastAPI serialization of Pydantic ORM mode blocking the event loop.

## Description

In the current implementation, if a *path operation function* returns a DB object with relations, and it is then serialized by Pydantic in ORM mode using `response_model`, the serialization is done in the main loop. And Pydantic with ORM mode would go and use the getters to retrieve the relationships (as it should, this is not Pydantic's fault), but that's a potentially (and most probably) I/O blocking operation, while it reads from the DB.

## Test it

To test the problem, run this sample application with Uvicorn:

```Python
import time
from typing import List

from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel


class DBItem:
    def __init__(self):
        self.name = "John"
        self.age = 20

    @property
    def pets(self):
        time.sleep(15)
        return ["Mr. Fluff", "Doge"]


class Item(BaseModel):
    name: str
    age: int
    pets: List[str]

    class Config:
        orm_mode = True


app = FastAPI()


@app.get("/")
async def read_main():
    return {"message": "Hello World"}


@app.get("/item", response_model=Item)
def read_item():
    item = DBItem()
    return item
```

It simulates a (very long) blocking operation with a `time.sleep()`.

Open several tabs at the same time with the docs UI and do 1 request to `/item` and the rest to `/`.

The request to `/item` will take 15 seconds to load (as it should). But the other requests will also take 15 seconds to load while they should be instantaneous. This is because they are all running in the same main loop.

## Fix it

Then apply this fix and try again. `/item` will take 15 seconds (as it should) but additional requests to `/` will be instantaneous.

## How it works

Whenever a *path operation function* uses normal `def` instead of `async def`, the model serialization is done in a threadpool.

There are no checks to see if the model uses ORM mode or not, as it's possible that the top-level model doesn't use ORM mode but a deeply nested sub-model could be using ORM mode.

This change looks correct to me, based on the [current v1.4 definition](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/blob/v1.4/pydantic/main.py#L736), and old [v0.32 definition](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/blob/v0.32.2/pydantic/main.py#L657).

I think this line is redundant now

I found removing line 99 still had the tests pass:
```diff
--- a/fastapi/utils.py
+++ b/fastapi/utils.py
@@ -96,7 +96,6 @@ def create_cloned_field(field: ModelField) -> ModelField:
         use_type = create_model(original_type.__name__, __base__=original_type)
         for f in original_type.__fields__.values():
             use_type.__fields__[f.name] = create_cloned_field(f)
-        use_type.__validators__ = original_type.__validators__
     if PYDANTIC_1:
         new_field = ModelField(
             name=field.name,
```
Nice, I found same.  Updated the branch and unit tests.

does adding this test new scenario? If the tests here don't trigger a failure case, I don't think adding these two validators adds anything, whereas testing for failure would make sure that the validators are inherited (worth checking in other places to make sure that isn't covered elsewhere, as this module seems to have a specific scenario in mind)

title: Add note about Body parameters
body:
Add a link to section containing the documentation for Body parameters, emphasizing that using Pydantic models is not mandatory.

It's a follow-up for issue #895. Me and another colleague had the same trouble, thinking that Pydantic models were mandatory to accept params in the request body.

Waiting for your feedback of course as I wrote this note quickly, just to initiate the PR.

> `Δ = absolute <relative> (impact)`, `ø = not affected`, `? = missing data`
> Powered by [Codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/900?src=pr&el=footer). Last update [55afb70...2fef7ed](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/900?src=pr&el=lastupdated). Read the [comment docs](https://docs.codecov.io/docs/pull-request-comments).
Thanks for your contribution! :tada: :cake:

I tripped on this as well. And I really think it would be awesome to have FastAPI work with properly annotated but otherwise "unmodified" python functions and this "Body(...)" departs from that and is quite ugly I would say.

May I suggest we change the @app.post to:

**- Suggestion 1**: add a "param-lookup" argument to the post decorator so that we could specify the order in which FastAPI will look for 'non-path' and non BaseModel descendants parameters. It could be "query, body", "body, query", "query" or "body". Current default is "query", but should be changed to "query, body".

**- Suggestion 2**: add a "fallback-to-body" argument to the decorator, so if a parameter is missing after looking for them in the query string, FastAPI would then check the request body for them.

If any of these suggestions sound ok, I could try developing it and submitting a pull request. Just let me know if it would be welcomed.

The "Body(...)" workaround also has the limitation described here: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/1011
(the parameters will be considered optional)

I think it's quite consistent to have `Path`, `Query`, `Body`, `Header` and `Cookie` parameters and to have __only one default__ when none of the above class/function is used, which is to consider it's a query parameter.

In any case, since the implicit behavior can become quite confusing with lots of parameters (default to query, pydantic models as body, params in the route as path, etc.), I think it's best to always be explicit and wrap your parameters in `Query`, `Body`, etc. (just my personal opinion!).

But I'm concerned by the issue your references, as I thought using `Body(...)` (with an ellipsis) would make the parameter mandatory, just as what is done for Query: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/query-params-str-validations/#make-it-required.

While pydantic and FastAPI will understand the parameter as being mandatory, linters and cpython will see it as optional and it is an error in python to have optional parameters preceeding non-optional parameters so it may force one to reorder parameters around.
Hence, we get into the business of "converting methods" to become FastAPI-friendly when I always thought that being able to use existing methods 'as-is' was a great appeal of FastAPI. 

Also, I really do not understand why it would be a bad idea to fallback to checking for parameters in the body, even if you choose to limit this behavior to post method and when the body content type is form or json.

May I suggest two more alternatives?

**Suggestion 3: Have the decorator accept kwargs like this:**

@app.post('/', a = Body(...), b = Path(...), c = Query(...))
async def test(a: str, b: int, c: bool):
    pass


**Suggestion 4: create type alias to indicate where the parameters will be found**

# somewhere on FastAPI codebase:
TPath = typing.Union
TBody = typing.Union
TQuery = typing.Union
THeader = typing.Union
TCookie = typing.Union

@app.post('/')
async def test(a: TBody[str], b: TPath[int], c: TQuery[bool]):
    pass

> **Legend** - [Click here to learn more](https://docs.codecov.io/docs/codecov-delta)
> `Δ = absolute <relative> (impact)`, `ø = not affected`, `? = missing data`
> Powered by [Codecov](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/908?src=pr&el=footer). Last update [55afb70...513ee3c](https://codecov.io/gh/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/908?src=pr&el=lastupdated). Read the [comment docs](https://docs.codecov.io/docs/pull-request-comments).

Thanks @luckydonald for your contribution and interest. :cake: 

But I think this would be better handled in a dependency that parses the incoming string as @dmontagu suggested in the issue. That should solve it without needing extra logic in FastAPI for that specific use case. Also, the default encoding for query parameters is `form`, so this could end up misguiding users as it wouldn't do all the extra logic necessary to document it as a custom encoding in OpenAPI. As it is more of a corner case, I think the best approach is to use a dependency that does the JSON decoding from the string in the query.

Thanks @dmontagu for the review and detailed analysis! :rocket:
review_comments:
I defer to @tiangolo, but I personally don't think we should add special handling for `JsonWrapper` here or below.

I don't believe this is a performance sensitive part of the codebase, but adding special handling for this type:

1. Adds an on-going maintenance burden where we have to have tests for `JsonWrapper` and non-`JsonWrapper` code paths
2. Adds an on-going maintenance burden where we need to handle this edge case any time we may want to refactor this code in the future
3. Opens the door for requests for special handling of other types in the future

Moreover, because pydantic now runs its tests against FastAPI, it could potentially make it more difficult for pydantic to refactor the `Json` class in the future.

I could be convinced otherwise if this was a common request, but so far that doesn't seem to be the case.

There may be alternative approaches to make this work though; I'll comment on them elsewhere.

I think it would be better if we could rework this function and/or code path (and the corresponding one below) in such a way that `Json[X]` fields would be handled properly.

In particular, it would be better if we could test a different (closely-related) condition that didn't require specifically checking for `Json[X]`-based field types. I don't know if such a condition exists or could be easily made to exist though.

-----

It may also just make sense to loosen the restriction on what is allowed as a path/query/header-parameter type; I defer to @tiangolo about that.

------

Another preferable approach, in my opinion, would be to just change the `Json`/`JsonWrapper` types in *pydantic* in such a way that `Json[X]` types pass the check you want to perform. I haven't thought enough about the `Json` type to know whether there is a reasonable change that could be made here.

Assuming there *is* a reasonable change, depending on what is required, it may be a breaking change requiring waiting until pydantic v2. But if such a change is possible (whether reasonable or not), there *may* be a simple way to accomplish your goal by subclassing the `Json` class and changing an attribute (you could call the subclass `QueryJson` or similar).

Is @tiangolo maintainer there as well which could make an change to pydantic for fastapi realistic?
In any case I'm not sure if Pydantic is the correct place needing a change.
After all Pydantic is handling the Json type very well, decoding json, and reading it into lists, dicts and if specified even into real models just fine.
Currently we are throwing away that capability by limiting it to just the Body as source type.

It's just FastAPI adding too restrictive code on top of pydantic, therefore I made an exception for the case we want to handle here, Json.

It might be possible to maybe loosen the existing code in a more general way, however I don't really understand the existing code _(a few comments here and there would be really nice!)_ to make any in depth changes.

I mean I was actually surprised how easy it was for me to get it working, and that I basically only needed to change 2 lines.

title: Fix body parsing
body:
Closes #914 

I believe this is the "correct" way to fix this issue, as the provided body could be invalid in a number of ways, and this should handle anything that json could get parsed into. (The error message also seems appropriate to me even for malformed bodies.)

There was actually already a test for this, but it only checked for a (malformed) body of `None`, not `[]`, so it was easy to add a test case as another parametric case.

title: Fix path encoding
body:
This PR adds a case to `jsonable_encoder` so that `pathlib.PurePath` objects are serializable. Related to https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/563.

I know there was some discussion in that thread about how to handle the OS-dependent `str` representations of `pathlib.Path` objects. Perhaps I'm misunderstanding something, but shouldn't users who need OS-agnostic treatment of paths be using `pathlib.Pure*Path` types to get `str` representations in their native form, no matter where their code is running?

/cc @devtud
