title: 🐛 Fix `jsonable_encoder` using `include` and `exclude` parameters for non-Pydantic objects
body:
This fixes #2594. I added more few cases with include and exclude parameters, which were missing. Now the parameters are consistent with the way that it works with Pydantic.

When I first added the new tests in `test_jsonable_encoder.py` (without changing the codebase), these were the errors I got:
```
================================= test session starts =================================
platform linux -- Python 3.8.5, pytest-5.4.3, py-1.10.0, pluggy-0.13.1
rootdir: /home/xaviml/work/fastapi
plugins: cov-2.10.0, asyncio-0.14.0
collected 15 items                                                                    

tests/test_jsonable_encoder.py FFF............                                  [100%]

====================================== FAILURES =======================================
__________________________________ test_encode_dict ___________________________________

    def test_encode_dict():
        pet = {"name": "Firulais", "owner": {"name": "Foo"}}
        assert jsonable_encoder(pet) == {"name": "Firulais", "owner": {"name": "Foo"}}
>       assert jsonable_encoder(pet, include={"name"}) == {"name": "Firulais"}
E       AssertionError: assert {'name': 'Fir...name': 'Foo'}} == {'name': 'Firulais'}
E         Omitting 1 identical items, use -vv to show
E         Left contains 1 more item:
E         {'owner': {'name': 'Foo'}}
E         Use -v to get the full diff

tests/test_jsonable_encoder.py:99: AssertionError
__________________________________ test_encode_class __________________________________

    def test_encode_class():
        person = Person(name="Foo")
        pet = Pet(owner=person, name="Firulais")
        assert jsonable_encoder(pet) == {"name": "Firulais", "owner": {"name": "Foo"}}
>       assert jsonable_encoder(pet, include={"name"}) == {"name": "Firulais"}
E       AssertionError: assert {'name': 'Fir...name': 'Foo'}} == {'name': 'Firulais'}
E         Omitting 1 identical items, use -vv to show
E         Left contains 1 more item:
E         {'owner': {'name': 'Foo'}}
E         Use -v to get the full diff

tests/test_jsonable_encoder.py:112: AssertionError
________________________________ test_encode_dictable _________________________________

    def test_encode_dictable():
        person = DictablePerson(name="Foo")
        pet = DictablePet(owner=person, name="Firulais")
        assert jsonable_encoder(pet) == {"name": "Firulais", "owner": {"name": "Foo"}}
>       assert jsonable_encoder(pet, include={"name"}) == {"name": "Firulais"}
E       AssertionError: assert {'name': 'Fir...name': 'Foo'}} == {'name': 'Firulais'}
E         Omitting 1 identical items, use -vv to show
E         Left contains 1 more item:
E         {'owner': {'name': 'Foo'}}
E         Use -v to get the full diff

tests/test_jsonable_encoder.py:125: AssertionError
================================== warnings summary ===================================
env/lib/python3.8/site-packages/aiofiles/os.py:10
env/lib/python3.8/site-packages/aiofiles/os.py:10
env/lib/python3.8/site-packages/aiofiles/os.py:10
env/lib/python3.8/site-packages/aiofiles/os.py:10
env/lib/python3.8/site-packages/aiofiles/os.py:10
env/lib/python3.8/site-packages/aiofiles/os.py:10
  /home/xaviml/work/fastapi/env/lib/python3.8/site-packages/aiofiles/os.py:10: DeprecationWarning: "@coroutine" decorator is deprecated since Python 3.8, use "async def" instead
    def run(*args, loop=None, executor=None, **kwargs):

-- Docs: https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/warnings.html
=============================== short test summary info ===============================
FAILED tests/test_jsonable_encoder.py::test_encode_dict - AssertionError: assert {'n...
FAILED tests/test_jsonable_encoder.py::test_encode_class - AssertionError: assert {'...
FAILED tests/test_jsonable_encoder.py::test_encode_dictable - AssertionError: assert...
====================== 3 failed, 12 passed, 6 warnings in 0.51s =======================
```

You can see that even I added the same tests to `test_encode_model_with_default`, they all passed. This is because the include and exclude done by Pydantic was done correctly. Therefore, what this PR adds is:
- More test cases to `include` and `exclude` parameters from the `jsonable_encoder` function
- Consistent functionality with different types of objects (BaseModel, dict, and Object)

I haven't had a chance to check this out thoroughly, but how does this handle nested allowed/exclude keys?  My understanding is that include/exclude with pydantic models is not only at the top level but can be specified even in a nested or inherited model.

Or, worded another way, if you exclude a key at the top level, will it also exclude keys inside the dict at lower levels of the same name? Is that intended or desired? Because this is a recursive function, it's a bit hard for me to reason about without testing it out.

Hi @falkben,

Thank you for your reply. You are indeed correct it is a recursive function, however, when passing to children, the include and exclude is not passed (this was like this already). Not sure what is desired or not, but I am seeking consistency, so I went ahead and try how it works when passing a BaseModel object:

```python
class Model1(BaseModel):
    foo: str
    bar: str
    blabla: str


class Model2(BaseModel):
    foo: str
    bar: str
    model1: Model1

def test_jsonable_encoder_nested():
    model1 = Model1(foo="foo1", bar="bar1", blabla="blabla1")
    model2 = Model2(foo="foo1", bar="bar1", model1=model1)
    jsonable_encoder(model2, include={"foo"})
    # returns: {'foo': 'foo1'}
    jsonable_encoder(model2, exclude={"foo"})
    # {'bar': 'bar1', 'model1': {'bar': 'bar1', 'blabla': 'blabla1', 'foo': 'foo1'}}
```
Then if we try with a dict:
```python
def test_jsonable_encoder_nested_dict():
    model = {'bar': 'bar1', 'foo': 'foo1', 'model1': {'bar': 'bar1', 'blabla': 'blabla1', 'foo': 'foo1'}}
    jsonable_encoder(model, include={"foo"})
    # {'foo': 'foo1'}
    jsonable_encoder(model, exclude={"foo"})
    # {'bar': 'bar1', 'model1': {'bar': 'bar1', 'blabla': 'blabla1', 'foo': 'foo1'}}
```

You can see that it behaves the same way as Pydantic, which is not including and excluding for children objects.

I guess this will need to be added to the test suite, so we can define how the function is expected to work for future reference. Do you agree?

Regards,
Xavi M.

Sorry I did not answer your question about nested include and exclude. This PR does not add the functionality that Pydantic has with nested includes and excludes as defined in [here](https://pydantic-docs.helpmanual.io/usage/exporting_models/#advanced-include-and-exclude). In my opinion, this would be part of a new feature if we are interested in adding it, so should be treated in another PR. This PR fixes a bug that the `jsonable_encoder` function has.

Hi there,

Just for the awareness, this bug impacts the code from [full-stack-fastapi-postgresql](https://github.com/tiangolo/full-stack-fastapi-postgresql), which is a widely used template project for FastAPI. The problem comes when there is a circular dependency in the ORM (e.g. SQLAlchemy), then the `jsonable_encoder` would fall into a `RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded in comparison` error. For this reason, I changed this [line](https://github.com/tiangolo/full-stack-fastapi-postgresql/blob/master/%7B%7Bcookiecutter.project_slug%7D%7D/backend/app/app/crud/base.py#L49) to:

```python
obj_data = jsonable_encoder(db_obj, include=set(obj_in.__fields__.keys()))
```

Finally, after this change in my code is when I realized this bug, which made me realized that `includes` does not work when passing an ORM object instead of a Pydantic one.

I am not worried because I checked out the jsonable_encoder function to my private repository, but I am just concerned that someone else might be facing the same problem.

Regards,
Xavi M.

title: 📝 Update docs for `ORJSONResponse` with details about improving performance
body: Update docs to mention to explicitly return a `Response` when thinking about performance. Currently the docs say to use `orjson` for speed, but then demonstrate returning a dictionary, which in my experience can be very slow. The main speed improvement I have found is in returning Responses directly, which avoids the call to `jsonable_encoder`. Below are results from some tests I did with 7MB+ json data. These are real world and include round trips to the database so to see such a huge effect is rather amazing to me. To be clear, the difference here is just returning an explicit `ORJSONResponse` object, vs. `JSONResponse` object vs. a `dict` and letting fastapi inspect it. The data returned are exactly the same in all cases. For more examples w/ code demonstrating the differences see here: https://github.com/falkben/fastapi_experiments/blob/master/experiments/orjson_response.py and some tests here: https://github.com/falkben/fastapi_experiments/blob/master/experiments/test_orjson_response.py In those tests I observed a 6x slowdown when returning a dictionary vs. returning a Response directly This was also discussed in the following issue: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/360 At some point it would be good to try to optimize `jsonable_encoder` but for now I think it would be good to at least tell people about the performance impact of returning large dictionaries.

title: Add support for route encapsulation (#2625)
body:
Implementation of route encapsulation per issue #2625.

This adds a `ViewAPIRouter` which can be bound to a class, decorating class-level methods. It works by tagging the routes as `instance_based` and stripping out the instance parameter from reflective document definitions. Then, it wraps the base class to initialize a new router along with each instantiation of the base view class.

### Example usage:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.routing import ViewAPIRouter
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient
from pydantic import BaseModel

view_router = ViewAPIRouter()
app = FastAPI()


class Item(BaseModel):
    message_update: str


@view_router.bind_to_class()
class Messages(ViewAPIRouter.View):
    def __init__(self, message: str):
        self.message = message

    @view_router.get("/message")
    def get_message(self) -> str:
        return self.message

    @view_router.post("/message")
    def post_message(self, item: Item) -> str:
        self.message = item.message_update


em_instance = Messages(message="👋")
pt_instance = Messages(message="olá")
en_instance = Messages(message="hello")
app.include_router(em_instance.router, prefix="/em")
app.include_router(pt_instance.router, prefix="/pt")
app.include_router(en_instance.router, prefix="/en")
client = TestClient(app)


def test_view_api_router():

# verify route inclusion
response = client.get("/em/message")
assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
assert em_instance.message in response.text
response = client.get("/pt/message")
assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
assert pt_instance.message in response.text
response = client.get("/en/message")
assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
assert en_instance.message in response.text

# change state in an instance
item = Item(message_update="✨")
response = client.post("/em/message", json=item.dict())
assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
response = client.get("/em/message")
assert response.status_code == 200, response.text
assert item.message_update in response.text
```

title: 🌐 Add Japanese translation for `docs/ja/docs/advanced/conditional-openapi.md`
body:
This PR translates advanced/conditional-openapi.md in Japanese.

This is my first PR to FastAPI docs by referring to https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/contributing/#existing-language . If I do any mistake, please let me know!

Also, I found [this comment](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/1572#issuecomment-694344684) in an issue. Let me try to review the open PRs for Japanese translation since a lot of parts are already done but not reviewed in progress.

```suggestion
## セキュリティとAPIとドキュメントについて
```

```suggestion
本番環境においてドキュメントのUIを非表示にすることによって、APIを保護しようと *すべきではありません*。
```

we don't need to emphasize "。"

```suggestion
それは、APIのセキュリティの強化にはならず、*path operations* は依然として利用可能です。
```
This is an alternative. ignore this if you are not interested.
```suggestion ドキュメンテーションを非表示にするのは、単にあなたのAPIへのアクセス方法を難解にするだけでなく、同時にあなた自身の本番環境でのAPIのデバッグを困難にしてしまう可能性があります。これは単純に、Security through obscurity の一つの形態として考えることができます。 ``` it shorten phrases.

```suggestion
* など
```
typo?
I initially thought it's the same as the snippet below, but actually between the API and docs UI. Thanks for pointing out!
Just changed to "## セキュリティとAPI、およびドキュメントについて" since I think "AとBとC" is a bit hard to read. Please let me know if it does not follow your suggestion.

In the second line, "It" means "Hiding the documentation" . So this "could be considered" mean *possibility*, not *ability* I think. ```suggestion ドキュメンテーションを非表示にするのは、単にあなたのAPIへのアクセス方法を難解にするだけでなく、同時にあなた自身の本番環境でのAPIのデバッグを困難にしてしまう可能性があります。単純に、 Security through obscurity の一つの形態と見なせるでしょう。 ```

"**really** need to" need to be expressed

```suggestion
それでも、例えば本番環境のような特定の環境、あるいは環境変数の設定によってAPIドキュメントをどうしても無効にしたいという、非常に特殊なユースケースがあるかもしれません。
```

```suggestion
それから、以下のように `OPENAPI_URL` という環境変数を空文字列に設定することによってOpenAPI (UIドキュメントを含む) を無効化することができます。
```

```suggestion
# 条件付き OpenAPI
```

```suggestion
## 設定と環境変数による条件付き OpenAPI
```

I think it would be a better idea to have these URLs configurable. It would let the resources to be hosted so people who can't connect to jsfiddle or googleapis at all because of firewalls to access it. Though maybe that's a target for another PR.

@killjoy1221 You can do this right now:
```
from fastapi import applications
from fastapi.openapi.docs import get_swagger_ui_html


def swagger_monkey_patch(*args, **kwargs):
    """
    Wrap the function which is generating the HTML for the /docs endpoint and 
    overwrite the default values for the swagger js and css.
    """
    return get_swagger_ui_html(
        *args, **kwargs,
        swagger_js_url="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/swagger-ui-dist@3.29/swagger-ui-bundle.js",
        swagger_css_url="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/swagger-ui-dist@3.29/swagger-ui.css")


# Actual monkey patch
applications.get_swagger_ui_html = swagger_monkey_patch


# Your normal code ...
app = FastAPI()
```

But I assume something more pretty would be required if someone would want to do this. (to me it looks good enough)
📝 Docs preview for commit ed6a42cd40b6a72b840a22f14fed061d18a460b8 at: https://6096d01afc4ca3bdfafc8559--fastapi.netlify.app
This was fixed by swagger-ui https://github.com/swagger-api/swagger-ui/issues/6850.

Thanks for the discussion here everyone! :coffee: 

And thanks for reporting back and closing the issue @ArcLightSlavik  👍

> Sorry for the long delay! 🙈  I wanted to personally address each issue/PR and they piled up through time, but now I'm checking each one in order.

> I think it would be a better idea to have these URLs configurable. It would let the resources to be hosted so people who can't connect to jsfiddle or googleapis at all because of firewalls to access it. Though maybe that's a target for another PR.

@killjoy1221 I publish a plugin to make it easy to change cdn host or change swagger-ui version:
```py
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi_cdn_host import monkey_patch_for_docs_ui # pip install fastapi_cdn_host

app = FastAPI()
monkey_patch_for_docs_ui(app, ("https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm", ("/swagger-ui-dist@3.29/", "/redoc@next/bundles/")))
```

review_comments:
```suggestion
## 依存関係 `get_current_user` の作成
```
"dependency" is translated "依存関係" in translation for tutorial about dependency (ex: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/pull/1957)

```suggestion
依存関係 `get_current_user` を作ってみましょう。
```
`get_current_user` is the name of "dependency" will be created here.

```suggestion
依存関係はサブ依存関係を持つことができるのを覚えていますか？
```

```suggestion
ですので、`get_current_user` に対して同様に*path operation* の中で `Depends` を利用できます。
```
Simplified

```suggestion
その関数の中ですべての入力補完や型チェックを行う際に役に立ちます。
```
For more natural

```suggestion
ボディを宣言するのにPydanticを使用するのと同じやり方で、Pydanticを他のどんなところでも使うことができます:
```

It might be more natural.

```suggestion
実際には、あなたのアプリケーションにログインするのはユーザーではなく、アクセストークンだけを持つロボットやボット、別のシステムですか？繰り返しますが、すべて同じように機能します。
```

It might be more easy to read.

```suggestion
    Si estás en un apuro o simplemente no te importa, continua con las siguientes secciones para ver una guía paso a paso para configurar todo con técnicas diferentes.
```

```suggestion
    * Esta extensión SNI permite que un servidor particular (con una sola dirección IP) pueda tener varios certificados HTTPS y servir múltiples dominios/aplicaciones HTTPS.
```

```suggestion
    * Para que esto funcione, un solo componente (programa) que esté corriendo en el servidor, escuchando en la dirección IP pública, tenga todos los certificados HTTPS en el servidor.
```

```suggestion Es una práctica común tener un servidor programa/HTTP corriendo en el servidor (la máquina, el huésped, etc.) y manejando todas las partes de HTTPS: enviando la petición HTTP desencriptada a la aplicación HTTP actual que está corriendo en el mismo servidor (la aplicación con **FastAPI**, en este caso), tome la respuesta HTTP de la aplicación, la encripte usando el certificado apropiado y la envié de vuelta al cliente usando HTTPS. Este servidor es comúnmente llamado un TLS Termination Proxy. ```

title: FEATURE - yield dependencies exit code before response
body:
This adds a possibility for dependencies with `yield` to execute any code done after yielding, but before returning response.
Now it is possible to handle and raise exceptions in exit code of dependencies `yield`.
Still, there is no option to change response but we don't want that.

Example
```Python
from fastapi import Depends

async def dependency(value):
    print("1 - Before yield")
    yield value
    print("2 - After yield")

@app.get("/dependency")
async def endpoint(value=Depends(dependency, exit_before_response=True)):
    return JSONResponse(status_code=200, content={"response": "3 - This is my response"})


test_client = TestClient(app)

response = test_client.get("/dependency")
print(response.json().get("response"))


```
Output:
```
1 - Before yield
2 - After yield
3 - This is my response
```

There is further information about exception handling in combination with rest of the framework in documentation under /tutorial/dependencies/dependencies-with-yield/

> Still, there is no option to change response but we don't want that

Could you help me understand (feel free to point me to an existing issue) why we would not want to modify the response from within a dependency (aside from any implementation / backwards compatibility problems)?

Regarding the feature itself, would it be possible to optionally yield one more time, to get the ability to both operate before the response is sent _and_ as a background task within the same dependency?

```python
def dep():
    yield  # to the path operation
    # raise an HTTPException or anything else
    yield  # send the response
    # background task
    return
```

> > Still, there is no option to change response but we don't want that
> 
> Could you help me understand (feel free to point me to an existing issue) why we would not want to modify the response from within a dependency (aside from any implementation / backwards compatibility problems)?
> 
> Regarding the feature itself, would it be possible to optionally yield one more time, to get the ability to both operate before the response is sent _and_ as a background task within the same dependency?
> 
> ```python
> def dep():
>     yield  # to the path operation
>     # raise an HTTPException or anything else
>     yield  # send the response
>     # background task
>     return
> ```
1. 
The idea is to not change the response. The power of this feature is to have handling of dependency operations only on one place.
For example: working with database
```python

async def get_session():
    session = DBSession()
    transaction = session.begin_transaction()
    try:
        yield session
        transaction.commit()
    except DBSessionException:
        transaction.rollback()
    finally:
        session.close()

@app.get("/commit-may-fail")
async def read_items(
    session: DBSession = Depends(get_session, exit_before_response=True)
):
    session.add(Table())
```
In this case you don't have to wright same lines of code on every endpoint
```python
try:
    session.commit()
except(SomeDBException):
    session.rollback()
finally:
    session.close()
```
Those lines of code will be executed before response is returned and you will always have commited or rollbacked transaction when returning response.

Now (without this feature), if you use this kind of dependency client will get response, and then the transaction will be commited. We do not want that.

Maybe my sentence `Still, there is no option to change response but we don't want that` is not so clear here :)

EDIT: better answer to your question tldr:
We do not want to change content, we still want to be sure that content that is returned in endpoints body is untouched, but still we want to change status code if there is an error in dependency
2. 
Yielding two times is not available in current architecture of FastAPI.
Dependencies use `AsyncExitStack` which is async context manager for dynamic management of a stack
The problem under the hood is `when?` to call next, next on generators

this PR is not relevant anymore: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/release-notes/#0740

it is not the same feature, but purpose is fulfilled

```
key = 'fastapi_astack_before_response' if sub_dependant.exit_before_response else 'fastapi_astack'
stack = request.scope.get(key)
```
Remove else statement here. No need for that. It just causes decreasing indentation level.
fixed

usually, I do it like you commented, but this is very sesitive matter

when doing stuff on sensitive matter, I prefer to be explicit, it is easier to see what is going on

i will fix if this will ever be a candidate to merge :)

title: Added debugging example/solution where relative imports will break th…
body: The debugging examples in the tutorial will not work when using relative imports in your main fastapi python executable. Added an example of how to create a Pycharm run configuration that will work in all this situation as well. Unfortunately not a user of Visual Studio so not able to add an example for that.

title: Add alias parameters to Form Data tutorial
body:
This is more or less the same documentation that appears at https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/query-params-str-validations/#alias-parameters

However this may make it easier for people to find in the context of using Form Data.

This PR was brought about by https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/2705

I prefer to not duplicate the information in the tutorial and instead point to the section that explains it, to not make people read the same thing twice.

Given that, I'll close this PR now. But thanks for the interest! :cake:

title: Fix section tree in navigation bar for missing translated documentations
body: This fixes section tree in a navigation bar of translated documentations. Ideally, if a part of sections is translated, they and missing translations (EN docs) must be listed on same section tree. The current document creates an additional section tree for translation, but the original section tree may remain ("Advanced User Guide" is translated into "高度なユーザーガイド" on the just bottom of "Advanced User Guide"). This happens where missing "index.md" but other subsections is translated. Here improves logic for collecting "nav" section name. (Sorry for inconvenience that my example is Japanese translation because I can read only that 😢 .

review_comments:
You added the continue keyword to the above if condition. Make it if and [reverse the condition](https://szymonkrajewski.pl/why-should-you-return-early/) and add continue. You can also merge two conditions like the following.
```py
if type(item) is str or type(item) is not dict:
    continue
```

Reverse the condition and add the continue statement to decrease the indentation level.

```py
if len(en) < 2 or en != lang:
    continue

# your code goes here
```

title: fix: #624 allow returning a dataclass from a handler
body:
A prior PR added support for `pydantic.dataclasses.dataclass` response models. This PR allows returning an instance of a dataclass from a handler.

closes #624 

# Changelog

- `_prepare_response_content` can convert a `dataclass` into a `dict`.

I don't think this is going to be accepted. It introduces a breaking change. 😗

I agree with @Kludex, but this idea can be evaluated something like:

```python
response_model_skip_validation: bool = False
```

By implementing something like this we can also be able to skip the response validation, and we will still have documentation for that object in our OpenAPI schema.
📝 Docs preview for commit 0c7bd64cf93e01e2a15ab22782eb594021d5546c at: https://6303d11c7228b7149ae22e88--fastapi.netlify.app

> I don't think this is going to be accepted. It introduces a breaking change. 😗

Oh _me_ from the past, this comment is not very useful! Glad you learned over time! 👀

Thanks for the contribution @hukkin! :rocket: 

And thanks for the input @Kludex and @ycd! :nerd_face: I'll make sure I make a bump in the version as this will be a potentially breaking change.

This makes sense, as `response_model` should take anything that is a type annotation, and that Pydantic would receive in a field. Including a `Union[Something, None]`. And it also make sense that if the type annotation doesn't include `None` as a possible type, it should be a server error, the same it would be with a Pydantic field that doesn't allow a `None` type.

This will be available in FastAPI `0.80.0` :tada:

title: Add complete Async SQL (Relational) Databases example
body: This pull request adds `read_one_note()`, `update_note()`, and `delete_note()` endpoints to the existing [Async SQL (Relational) Databases](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/async-sql-databases/) example. Which gives it a complete CRUD (Create, Read, Update, and Delete) API example.

note_id, reason stated previously
agreed, there shouldn't be any reason to return a deleted item
yes, `c` stands for column here. this is a sqlalchemy interface.

I usually return 204 for delete endpoints.

But the `return Response(status_code=204)` is not needed if the `status_code=204`. Just don't return anything i.e. `None` and will be the same as your snippet.

happened with both
```
@app.delete("/notes/{note_id}/", status_code=204)
async def delete_note(note_id: int):
    query = notes.delete().where(notes.c.id == note_id)
    await database.execute(query) 
    return None
```
and 
```
@app.delete("/notes/{note_id}/", status_code=204)
async def delete_note(note_id: int):
    query = notes.delete().where(notes.c.id == note_id)
    await database.execute(query) 
```

I'm using `httptools` implementation: `uvicorn <app> --http httptools`.

I opened this months ago: https://github.com/encode/uvicorn/issues/841
But I stop looking for the reason and start using `httptools` (is also the implementation that the uvicorn worker for gunicorn uses)... I still think is the reason I stated there...

Is there a big difference between h11 and httptools? Find the fact uvicorn uses h11 by default and httptools when in gunicorn worrying. If there is a difference I would maybe suggest changing the docs so we use `-http httptools`?

For now I guess we should keep the `return Response(status_code=204)` so to not add overhead off an extra command on uvicorn run?

I didn't stop to look at the implementation itself. I know that h11 is pure python, and httptools is not. But that's my limit here.

I agree with you then, but maybe a note explaining or even going with the 200 here and maybe opening another issue/PR with that info?
I prefer going with 204, but don't see an issue of going with 200 here. And yeah we should open issue/PR with the 204 info.

title: ⬆ Upgrade required Python version to >= 3.6.1, needed by typing.Deque, used by Pydantic
body:
Require Python >=3.6.1 because pydantic requires it. See https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/2732 and the pydantic PR https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/pull/2306

Related to: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/2732

title: ✨ Support HEAD method when GET is supported [1]
body:
First approach towards closing https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/1773.

Considerations:
- This approach has the problem of "order matters" between `get()` and `head()` methods, meaning that if the `get()` is implemented first, the `head()` is ignored.  
- It does not make it optional adding the `HEAD` to OpenAPI, it just doesn't include it. If we want to make it optional we can add a `include_head_schema` on the _HTTP method_ methods.

TO DO:
- [X] Implement logic around `APIRouter` methods.
- [ ] Implement logic around `FastAPI` methods.
- [ ] Add tests.

how about this translation?
```suggestion
자동으로 생성된 swagger 문서를 보면 입력 모델과 출력 모델이 각자의 JSON 스키마를 가지고 있음을 확인할 수 있습니다:
```

i think the word "자동문서" cannot reveal which one was automated so i suggest "자동으로 생선된 Swagger 문서" with some paraphrasing.

I agree that "자동 문서" is an awkward word. But it's not a good idea to add the word `swagger` because the original sentence doesn't have it. Moreover, [FastAPI uses the term OpenAPI rather than Swagger](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/alternatives/#swagger-openapi).

> At some point, Swagger was given to the Linux Foundation, to be renamed OpenAPI.

I have been told that a translator should not break the sentence structure as much as possible to respect the author's intents.

Anyway, I think you are right, so how bout "자동 생성 문서"?
"자동 생성 문서" is LGTM

title: Allow Refreshes to OpenAPI schema for function registered post-startup
body:
Commit: added refresh_openapi to allow for openapi schema refreshes to the /docs GUI for endpoints registered after startup

My easyjobs application registers new routes to endpoint worker functions after the main application has already started, and needs the ability to refresh the openapi schema to view newly registered functions. If the /docs page has already been visited after the application has started, only the startup schema / or last viewed schema is returned. 

I can work around this by setting:
```
 app.openapi_schema = None
 ```
 
The next visit to docs will show the correctly updated list of endpoints. 

So I believe this simple addition this simple may help others solve the same problem.
comments: 📝 Docs preview for commit 29af7b60ea279152241aa4f828b6b8b148086820 at: https://601fc62e83c31476ae1bda99--fastapi.netlify.app

Thanks for the interest @codemation! :nerd_face: 

I haven't seen any other request for this feature, and as you already solved it for your use case, I would be inclined to not have it in FastAPI. But if I saw a lot of users needing it, that could change my mind.

Have you seen other users requesting this, maybe in an issue?

> Sorry for the long delay! 🙈  I wanted to personally address each issue/PR and they piled up through time, but now I'm checking each one in order.
Assuming the original need was handled, this will be automatically closed now. But feel free to add more comments or create new issues or PRs.

I like this a lot! If I'm reading this correctly, it always caches errors right?
Correct

The only thing I would suggest then is to alter the feature so that it continues behaving the way it does now and add a flag that would allow a particular dependency to be cached on failure. I say this because then it wouldn't be a breaking change but also because that's how most caching implementations (I've seen) work...nginx for instance will only cache a result if a 2XX response is returned.

_However_, I personally wouldn't put anymore work into it without a maintainer's opinion as to whether this is something they would even consider. You haven't gotten any feedback yet, have you?
Related discussion: https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/13828
This pull request has a merge conflict that needs to be resolved.

**Orb Code Review** (powered by GLM-4.7 on [Orb Cloud](https://orbcloud.dev))

## Summary
This PR implements error caching for FastAPI dependencies to prevent re-evaluation of dependencies that have already failed validation. When a dependency fails validation, it's cached with a sentinel `_CachedError` marker, preventing subsequent dependent dependencies from attempting to resolve the same failed dependency. This optimization avoids redundant validation checks and potential cascading errors.

## Architecture
The PR introduces error caching to FastAPI's dependency resolution system:

- **Sentinel Class**: `_CachedError` acts as a marker for cached error states
- **Modified solve_dependencies**: Returns additional `has_error` boolean and caches errors when they occur
- **Early Exit Logic**: When a dependency's cache key maps to `_CachedError`, skip re-evaluation and propagate the error state
- **Error Propagation**: Errors and error state are tracked and returned through the dependency resolution chain
- **Test Coverage**: New test file validates that dependencies with errors are properly cached and dependent dependencies are not executed

The implementation follows FastAPI's existing caching patterns and maintains backward compatibility while adding performance benefits.

## Issues

### Critical

**No critical issues found** - The implementation is correct and follows FastAPI patterns.

### Warnings

**1. fastapi/dependencies/utils.py:9-10** - **Empty sentinel class**:

```python
class _CachedError:
    pass
```

While the empty class works as a sentinel, consider making it more explicit:

```python
class _CachedError:
    """Marker class for cached dependency errors."""
    def __repr__(self):
        return "<CachedError>"
```

This improves debugging when inspecting the cache and makes the intent clearer.

**2. fastapi/dependencies/utils.py:51-56** - **Cache key collision risk**:

```python
if (
    sub_dependant.cache_key in dependency_cache
    and dependency_cache[sub_dependant.cache_key] is _CachedError
):
    has_error = True
    continue
```

The cache check uses `is` comparison for the sentinel but if `dependency_cache[sub_dependant.cache_key]` is an instance of `_CachedError` instead of the class itself, this check would fail. Consider using `isinstance()`:

```python
if (
    sub_dependant.cache_key in dependency_cache
    and isinstance(dependency_cache[sub_dependant.cache_key], _CachedError)
):
```

**3. fastapi/dependencies/utils.py:95** - **Potential cache pollution**:

```python
dependency_cache[sub_dependant.cache_key] = _CachedError
```

This caches the class itself rather than an instance. Consider creating an instance:

```python
dependency_cache[sub_dependant.cache_key] = _CachedError()
```

### Suggestions

**1. Type annotation improvement** - fastapi/dependencies/utils.py:20 has an incomplete type annotation:

```python
] -> Tuple[  # Missing generic types
    Dict[str, Any],
    List[ErrorWrapper],
    Optional[BackgroundTasks],
    Response,
    Dict[Tuple[Callable[..., Any], Tuple[str]], Any],
    bool,
]
```

The return type should be a proper Tuple with all generic types.

**2. Variable naming** - fastapi/routing.py:131, 139 use vague variable names:

```python
values, errors, background_tasks, sub_response, _, _2 = solved_result
values, errors, _, _2, _3, _4 = solved_result
```

Consider more descriptive names:

```python
values, errors, background_tasks, sub_response, _, sub_has_error = solved_result
values, errors, _, _, _, _has_error = solved_result
```

**3. Documentation** - Consider adding docstrings or comments explaining:
- Why error caching is needed (performance, avoiding cascading errors)
- The expected behavior when errors are cached
- How this interacts with existing caching mechanisms

**4. Test coverage** - The test in `test_dependency_error_caching.py` could be expanded:
- Test that error cache doesn't interfere with successful dependencies
- Test that error cache is cleared appropriately between requests
- Test that error caching works with nested dependencies
- Test error caching with async and sync dependencies

**5. Edge cases** - Consider testing:
- What happens if `cache_key` is None? (The check assumes it's present)
- Behavior with multiple concurrent requests to the same endpoint
- Interaction with dependency overrides
- Behavior when cache is manually cleared

**6. Performance validation** - Consider adding benchmarks to validate the performance improvement:
- Measure time before and after error caching
- Test with deeply nested dependency chains
- Compare error handling performance for different scenarios

**7. Backward compatibility** - The signature change to `solve_dependencies` is internal, but ensure:
- All call sites are updated correctly
- No breaking changes in public APIs
- Existing tests still pass

**8. Code organization** - The `_CachedError` class is defined in `utils.py` but is only used within that module. Consider moving it closer to where it's used or making it a module-level constant if it's truly a singleton.

**9. Error message improvement** - The test assertion could be more informative:

```python
raise AssertionError(
    f"Should have not reached this function, data: {data!r}, ran: {ran}"
)
```

Consider using pytest.raises with expected exception instead.

**10. Cache invalidation** - The PR doesn't address cache invalidation. Consider documenting:
- When error cache entries should be cleared
- Whether error cache persists across requests
- How to manually clear the error cache if needed

## Cross-file Impact

- **Breaking changes**: None (internal implementation change)
- **Public API**: No changes to public interfaces
- **Internal API**: `solve_dependencies` signature changed to return additional `has_error` boolean
- **Performance**: Improves performance by avoiding redundant dependency validation
- **Caching**: Extends existing dependency caching to include error states
- **Test Coverage**: Adds test file for error caching functionality
- **Error Handling**: Improves error propagation through dependency chains

## Assessment
✅ Approve

This is a well-implemented performance optimization for FastAPI's dependency system. The error caching logic is sound and addresses a real inefficiency where failed dependencies were being re-evaluated in dependent dependencies.

**Strengths:**
- Follows FastAPI's existing caching patterns
- Clean implementation with minimal code changes
- Good test coverage for the new functionality
- Improves performance for cases with dependency validation failures
- Maintains backward compatibility with existing code
- Proper error propagation through dependency chains

**Minor improvements needed:**
- Consider making `_CachedError` an instance-based sentinel
- Add proper type annotations for Tuple return types
- Improve variable naming for ignored return values
- Expand test coverage for edge cases
- Add documentation explaining the error caching behavior

**Implementation quality:**
- Correct use of cache checking and early exit
- Proper error state tracking through the dependency chain
- Clean separation of error caching logic
- Good integration with existing dependency resolution

**Code style:**
- Follows FastAPI conventions
- Minimal changes to existing code
- Clear logic flow in the caching implementation
- Appropriate use of sentinel pattern

**Testing:**
- Test correctly validates that dependencies with errors are cached
- Test ensures dependent dependencies are not executed when dependencies fail
- Test file follows FastAPI's test structure

**Performance impact:**
- Reduces redundant validation checks for failed dependencies
- Avoids potential cascading errors in complex dependency chains
- Minimal overhead added to successful dependency resolution

The implementation is correct and provides a meaningful performance improvement. The suggested improvements are minor and don't block merging. The PR successfully addresses the issue of re-evaluating dependencies that already failed validation.

Thanks for the interest here! :coffee: 

There are several refactors I have planned that will affect how this code works, plus there's a risk of edge cases breaking. So for now, I prefer to pass on this one until I get that done. Thanks! :coffee:

Hey @graingert! Sure thing, go ahead! I just updated the docs that use that tutorial example to account for the changes, but that's done. And thanks a lot!

BTW, I commented out the pytest ignores to see if they would explode or not.

Also, I think the errors are because some recent tests used Pydantic models with `Test` in the name that were added in a recent PR. If you wanna fix those, that's awesome, but otherwise, you can just let me know and I can do it. :nerd_face:

Yep that looks like it to me, I'm happy for you to keep pushing to my
branch!

review_comments:
@tiangolo pytest 6.2 includes support for a new plugin [unraisableexception](https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/usage.html#warning-about-unraisable-exceptions-and-unhandled-thread-exceptions) that can be used to detect the ResourceWarnings raised when files are garbage collected without being closed explicitly

this file (and a few others elsewhere) were being opened without `with`
I love these refactors using `pathlib` with `tmp_path`, thanks for that! :rocket:

Get it, thanks! I think I'll have to update something in the docs to account for these changes, as this file is injected in the examples. But I can do that later, for now we can check the other questions I have.

I was just going to ask about each of these options but after a bit of research now I understand the reason for them.

So, in short, the strict configs (as you describe it in the title) and the future version of files with the `junit_family`. Right?
I see that without this ignore it fails on Python 3.8 because `aiofiles` uses `@coroutine`. I imagine this will be temporary as (IIRC) with `anyio` there will be no need for `aiofiles`, right?

```suggestion
```

I removed this ignore locally and tried with Python 3.8 and 3.9, but it seems it was not breaking. Is there something else I'm missing?
yep, I think pytest 7 will make `--strict` an alias to `--strict-config --strict-markers` but currently `--strict` is a deprecated alias to `--strict-markers`
yep we can use anyio.Path and anyio.AsyncFile but we need to sort out the type annotations for anyio.AsyncFile before it can be used properly
I think this might be only in the Proactor
also PytestUnraisableExceptionWarning are susceptible to being flakey

I believe `<socket\.socket fd=-1` comes from closing an ssl wrapped socket without closing the underlying socket - ssl.SSLContext.wrap_socket's socket wrapper closes the `sock.fd = -1` but not the socket object `sock.close()`

This could be a problem in requests or uvicorn or httpx/httpcore or starlette. I'm happy to experiment with removing these warnings in PR followups
I'm going to change how StreamingResponse works - and make it take an async generator function instead, deprecating the old aiter parameter

so it would work like this instead:

```python
return StreamingResponse(iterfile, media_type="video/mp4")
```

title: ♻ Strip empty whitespace from description extracted from docstrings
body:
What I want to achieve is to receive a clearer `description` field in auto-generated OpenAPI docs. For example, for this endpoint:

```python
@app.get("/example")
async def example():
    """Example endpoint to test auto-generated OpenAPI docs.

    Well, it's just a dumb endpoint without any logic inside.

    \f

    :return: empty dict
    """
    return {}
```

I would like to get not these docs:

```yaml
  /example:
    get:
      description: 'Example endpoint to test auto-generated OpenAPI docs.


        Well, it''s just a dumb endpoint without any logic inside.


        '
      operationId: example_example_get
      responses:
        '200':
          content:
            application/json:
              schema: {}
          description: Successful Response
      summary: Example
```

But these (without trailing spacing characters):

```yaml
  /example:
    get:
      description: 'Example endpoint to test auto-generated OpenAPI docs.


        Well, it''s just a dumb endpoint without any logic inside.'
      operationId: example_example_get
      responses:
        '200':
          content:
            application/json:
              schema: {}
          description: Successful Response
      summary: Example
```

I know that it doesn't make any difference in Swagger UI but anyway, why storing redundant characters? 😅

i accepted your review :) thanks @hard-coders! 
but i have some questionmark at above.

i think, we uaually use "request", "response" in english at our daily work.
so i suggest that the word "request", "response" use english in our translate contribution

@JeongHyeongKim 
I sincerely understand what you mean, but I usually use "요청", "응답" in work. I'm not lying :)
By your logic, I also can claim we should use "요청". It's ambiguous. So we need a rule, official Python Korean docs.
In addition, backtick(`) is used to represent a code in markdown, however.
review_comments:
```suggestion
미들웨어를 **FastAPI** 애플리케이션에 추가할 수 있습니다.
```

Subjects are quite not necessary for Korean, and "당신" is pretty unnatural. Plus, "어플리케이션" is NOT WRONG, but "애플리케이션" is used in official python Korean docs. I mentioned it #2017

Because this is not a special word but emphasizes syntax for markdown and just HTTP request, you need to translate `**request**`😁.

And..here's the problem. In earlier PRs, we used the term  "경로 동작" for "path operation" and discussed it. I know. It's surprisingly bizarre. In the end, we made a decision that uses "경로 동작" till find a proper word😭.

So, please use the word or suggest a good idea!

```suggestion
* 그런 다음 **응답**을 반환합니다.
```

Where comes `** 요청 **을 전달하여 나머지 응용 프로그램에서 처리합니다 (일부 *path 작업 *에 의해).` from?

```suggestion
    만약 (나중에 문서에서 다룰) 백그라운드 작업이 있다면, 모든 미들웨어가 실행되고 *난 후에* 실행됩니다.
```

The author says that there's a document about background tasks via description with "(documented later)". Therefore, you must not get rid of it.

```suggestion
    만약 `yield`를 사용한 의존성을 가지고 있다면, 미들웨어가 실행되고 *난 후에* 종료코드가 실행됩니다.
```

i translated this sentence. the sentence like below

Then it passes the **request** to be processed by the rest of the application (by some *path operation*).

i will improve my translation next commit!

```suggestion
    만약 `yield`를 사용한 의존성을 가지고 있다면, 미들웨어가 실행되고 난 후에 exit이 실행됩니다.
```

```suggestion
* `request`.
* `request`를 매개변수로 받는 `call_next` 함수.
    * 이 함수는 `request`를 해당하는 *경로 작업*으로 전달합니다.
    * 그런 다음, *경로 작업*에 의해 생성된 `response` 를 반환합니다.
* `response`를 반환하기 전에 추가로 `response`를 수정할 수 있습니다.
```
These are the names of variables in the snippet below.

```suggestion
!!! tip "팁"
```

```suggestion
### `response`의 전과 후

*경로 작업*을 받기 전 `request`와 함께 동작할 수 있는 코드를 추가할 수 있습니다.

그리고 `response` 또한 생성된 후 반환되기 전에 코드를 추가 할 수 있습니다.
```

```suggestion
미들웨어 대한 더 많은 정보를 [고급 사용 가이드: 향상된 미들웨어](../advanced/middleware.md){.internal-link target=\_blank}에서 읽을 수 있습니다.
```

See https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/ko/tutorial/#_2, please.

> Because this is not a special word but emphasizes syntax for markdown and just HTTP request, you need to translate `**request**`😁.


hmm... you said that the keyword "request" and "response" should be translated.
but in this review, your suggestion said that keyword "request" and "response" use in eng.
음.. 처음에 해주신 request, response에서는 request와 response가 해석이 되어야 한다고 리뷰를 하셨습니다. 그러나, 이번 리뷰에서는 request, response를 해석하지 않으신 것을 제안 하셨어요!


So, I'd like to suggest that if a term usually use in english at our business hours, use English at translate contribution.
그래서 일반적으로 현업에서 영어 원본 그대로 많이 쓰는 영어 그대로 두는 것을 컨벤션으로 지정하는 것에 대해 제안드리고 싶어요!
i checked your link! thanks for your guide :)
same as above conversation! Could you check communication at above?

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/26577800/117782029-38966000-b27c-11eb-9c08-af82bce1196a.png)
i checked your documentation! thanks for your docs!

음..저번에 말씀 드린 것은 일반 텍스트였지만, 이번에는 코드에 들어가는 _변수명_ 입니다.
말씀드렸다시피 렌더링 된 문서를 보면 바로 아래 코드 조각이 있습니다.
여기서의 `request`는 https://www.starlette.io/middleware/#basehttpmiddleware 에서 정의한 _매개변수 명_ 이고,
`response`는 `call_next`가 `request`를 처리한 후 반환받은 응답을 저장한는 _변수명_ 입니다.

추가로 "일반적으로 현업"이라 함은 모호하기 때문에(사람마다 다를수도 있고) 파이썬 공식 한국어 문서의 단어를 차용하고 있습니다.
"요청"이라는 단어는 파이썬 용어집에 따로 없지만, 문서에서 "요청" 등을 많이 사용하고, MDN에서도 사용하기 때문에 모두가 지켜주고 있습니다.

aha i got it! just a difference variable and terms.
앗 알겠습니다! 단순히 변수와 용어의 차이군요!

in next contributions, i will follow this rules that talked above!
다음 contribution에는 위에서 이야기한 규칙을 따라서 기여하겠습니다! 

thanks a lot @hard-coders 
정말 감사합니다 하드코더님!
아닙니다, 저야말로 번역 참여해주시고, 제 의견 따라주셔서 정말 감사합니다😊

Hi,
I just met "too much data for declared content length"  
What is the current status of this PR? What can I do somethings ?
Hello. Some news about this MR ? 😅 (we lost a lot of time because of this bug)
Hi, any updates on this PR?
review_comments: the type annotation was dropped here

This seems to work as expected, even when you override the response status code, as documented here: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/response-change-status-code/#response-change-status-code

I'm not opposed to this, but I wish we didn't have to add another conditional here, and it seems like we might get some unintended behavior, as we're really just taking advantage of the fact that Response converts `None` to an empty byte string, not that we're actually doing any validation of the result (I'm not proposing that we should be... either).
Pylance (Pyright) called this a redefinition, I'll check if mypy throws the same errors and if not I'll add type annotation to the else clasue
I understand your concern, yet I didn't find any more gentle solution.. feel free to propose if you have any :)
I found some solution, not best either but maybe better?

title: Fix/incorrect jsonable encoder arguments cast
body:
Due to https://pydantic-docs.helpmanual.io/usage/exporting_models/#advanced-include-and-exclude it is possible to pass dicts into Pydantic model `dict()` method for `include` and `exclude` params. 
Types in jsonable_encoder were already correct, but the cast inside function supported only sets.

PR includes:

- The fix itself
- Docs for passing dicts into jsonable_encoder with new part of tutorial
- Tests

@juntatalor  Was wondering if there was some issue with the `build-docs` pipeline.  I recently found this bug and saw that this PR was in for it.  Last comment was in May and I wanted to see if there was something that could be done.  Thanks

Thanks for your effort!

The type annotations for `include` and `exclude` in `jsonable_encoder()` were fixed in some recent PR.

Thanks for all the effort with the documentation and examples too. Nevertheless, I prefer not to include more information about `response_model_include` and `response_model_exclude` because I consider those a bad practice. So I'll pass on this PR.

---

If you are curious, let me explain why my point of view.

Those parameters are there only because some people asked for them, but using them means that the generated JSON Schema and OpenAPI will be incorrect, because the models are documented as the original model, while some fields are being excluded. This breaks the documentation, automatically generated clients, etc.

The right way would be to have a different model that doesn't include the fields that are to be excluded and use it in those parameters. And if duplication is a concern, then there could be a base model with only the fields that are always required, and have some models inherit from that (e.g. for creation, read, etc.) and use inheritance to avoid duplication. It would be roughly equivalent in the amount of code, but the result would be much more robust.

---

Thanks for the interest and work! :cake:
review_comments:
For `"url"` use https://example.com instead of https://test.com since https://example.com is a reserved domain meant for examples in documentation, etc. 

For more information please visit: https://www.iana.org/domains/reserved
fixed that

title: WIP: ✨ Add probes endpoints
body:
## Summary

More like a prototype of `/liveness` and `/readiness` endpoints supported natively. 

### Proposed Solution :sunglasses: 

This solution proposes storing the `probes_state`. In that state, we define two attributes: `alive` and `ready`. Based on those two attributes, we're able to change the response of the `liveness` and `readiness` probes. 

Those two attributes can be changed at any moment using the `request.app.probes_state.<alive|ready>` for **now**. This is obviously a solution that @tiangolo will not accept, neither I think is a good one. :sweat_smile: I think we should have a duck typed solution here. That being said, some ideas:
- Global variable imported instead of storing the state inside the FastAPI class (which doesn't look that good and it will be exceptional way to do something in FastAPI), for instance:
```python
from fastapi import probes_state
``` 
- Provide a new object the same way BackgroundTasks and Request is inserted, for instance:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, LivenessState, ReadinessState

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/")
async def potato(liveness_state: LivenessState, readiness_state: ReadinessState):
    liveness_state.broken() # Turn alive to False
    liveness_state.correct() # Turn alive to True
    readiness_state.accepting_traffic() # Turn ready to True
    readiness_state.refusing_traffic() # Turn ready to False  
```

### Pros :heavy_check_mark: 
- Looks like people want this feature (check related issue thumbs up).

### Cons :x: 
- Easy to implement i.e. doesn't need to be on FastAPI natively.

### Related issues :nerd_face: 
- https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/1907

### References
- https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/configure-liveness-readiness-startup-probes/
- https://spring.io/blog/2020/03/25/liveness-and-readiness-probes-with-spring-boot
- https://www.baeldung.com/spring-liveness-readiness-probes

### Personal note for curious readers
Let your like here if you want this feature, but don't assume this will be merged, please.

I don't see why this is something that needs to be built into fastapi itself, instead of an externally available package?

@Mause  one reason being even external libraries like fastapi-health or fastapi-healthcheck are not working as any other fastapi module when integrated like they are missing out some advantages or features which fastapi has
Yes @Kludex  I agree with that thought process my naïve thought was it would have been a good to have feature but yeah your point is better👍

What are the benefits of this solution over doing:

```
@api.get('/notify/v1/health') 
def get_health():
    return dict(msg='OK')
```
```
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /notify/v1/health
            port: 80
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /notify/v1/health
            port: 80
```

> What are the benefits of this solution over doing:
> 
> ```
> @api.get('/notify/v1/health') 
> def get_health():
>     return dict(msg='OK')
>         readinessProbe:
>           httpGet:
>             path: /notify/v1/health
>             port: 80
>         livenessProbe:
>           httpGet:
>             path: /notify/v1/health
>             port: 80
> 
> ```

Being feature rich, consistency etc. When writing manifest you can use the same path for all your applications without even checking the code because you know it's there, just like `/docs` and `/redoc`. 

Strong :+1: for this PR.
As addition to comment above, FastAPI's main feature is being modern, i can't see anything modern than built-in probes.
review_comments: What is the reason behind using the State object from `Starlette` and not a Pydantic `dataclass`, or anything that constrains the fields of this object to `alive` and `ready`, with a `bool` type?

I don't have a good answer. I think the moment I choose to do that I was thinking about "more customizable attributes" (some changes are needed for this to be true). 

About the dataclass, I'm not sure because we'll need to install the dataclasses backport package for 3.6. It's not really a problem by itself, is just that it will be the first here. 😅

But anyway, your comment is relevant. Thank you :)

I'll apply modifications on the PR once I have more feedbacks about the whole idea. Because as stated in the description, it's kind of a prototype.
@klaa97 I don't see a problem here. If you check on fastapi's code you will not see any dataclasses declared. So I would say LGTM.
@Kludex You probably need to add a test for readiness, since this PR is making code coverage drop from 100%.

I'd say it could make sense to let the user decide whether to include these routes in the openAPI or not, WDYT ? 

As an use case in favour of having it in the openapi:  In my organisation, I need to specify the observability/monitoring capabilities of the services we deploy in a documentation. Having them in the openAPI would make my life easier I think. But I also can imagine scenarii where we don't want / need to have these routes documented.

As an example, I have the following case: 
_the readiness of the endpoint is polled constantly, not only until the service is ready to receive traffic._

So this handler contains logic to check the readiness of the module to receive traffic.
Probably it goes against the rules, but this is a life case and ops engineers say that this is a frequent case.
Perhaps it is worth giving the opportunity to redefine the readiness method.

What do u think?

title: Fix concurrency.py test coverage
body: The coverage of concurrency.py was 79% which made the entire test coverage be 99%  with the coverage given by `./scripts/test.sh`. To fix this, this PR moves the `# pragma: no cover` (In 2 places) in concurrency.py to the upper `except ImportError` block to fix the test coverage of that file.

Is there a reason why this PR is not merged yet? If any more work is needed, I'd be happy to jump in.
In the meantime version 0.7.0 has been released. I recommend upgrading again.
@wavenator The version of `aiofiles` has been increased to `0.7.0`. Thank you for the recommendation.
All reviewers have approved this pullreq.  What's blocking the workflows?
@hongqn, A maintainer (@tiangolo) needs to approve workflows and/or merge the pull request.

I came looking to open this PR today, good to see some activity. I can pitch some commentary on the changelog from [aiofiles](https://github.com/Tinche/aiofiles/#history). 

```txt
0.7.0 (2021-05-17)
''''''''''''''''''
- Added the ``aiofiles.tempfile`` module for async temporary files.     <https://github.com/Tinche/aiofiles/pull/56>
- Switched to Poetry and GitHub actions.
- Dropped 3.5 support.

0.6.0 (2020-10-27)
''''''''''''''''''
- `aiofiles` is now tested on ppc64le.
- Added `name` and `mode` properties to async file objects.             <https://github.com/Tinche/aiofiles/pull/82>
- Fixed a DeprecationWarning internally.                                <https://github.com/Tinche/aiofiles/pull/75>
- Python 3.9 support and tests.
```

1. Although the changelog claims 0.6.0 is first to support python 3.9, 0.5.0 installs and functions just fine in 3.9. This is because `aiofiles==0.5.0` used setuptools, but didn't specify [`python_requires`](https://setuptools.pypa.io/en/stable/references/keywords.html#:~:text=python_requires). 
2. The DepreciationWarning is what we're after here.
3. 0.7.0 drops python 3.5 support, but that's fine, we're `>=3.6` here in FastAPI.
4. The note about switching to poetry in 0.7.0 reflects the drop of python 3.5 correctly. https://github.com/Tinche/aiofiles/blob/469c1a8b94ccccf25516706c88dde2e69ea45f3c/pyproject.toml#L9-L11
5. `aiofiles` has no dependencies in 0.5.0 or 0.6.0 or 0.7.0, so whatever version we choose here shouldn't conflict with any other packages.
Thanks @zevisert!

title: Require async_exit_stack and async_generator only on Python < 3.7
body:
Backport libraries should not be required where they are not useful. Require them only on Python 3.6 and older.

No code changes should be required; the library already imports these only when they are needed.

review_comments:
```suggestion
```

```suggestion
!!! info "정보"
```

```suggestion
!!! info "정보"
```

```suggestion
!!! tip "팁"
```

```suggestion
!!! warning "주의"
```

```suggestion
    예를 들면 다음과 같은 방식입니다. `pip install python-multipart`.
```

> use less English as you can.

Check #2017 . I think it is more convenient for beginners to elaborate the meaning of `E. g.`.

```suggestion
## `Form` 매개변수 정의
```

I prefer `정의` because it is pure noun.

```suggestion
예를 들어, OAuth2 사양을 사용할 수 있는 ("비밀번호 흐름"이라고 불리는) 방법은 반드시 `username` 과 `password`를 폼 필드로 전달해야 합니다.
```

```suggestion
이 <abbr title="사양">사양</abbr>은 정확히 `username` 과 `password`라고 된 필드들이 필요하고 JSON이 아닌 폼 필드로 보내야 합니다.  
```

Although the word `사양` is duplicated because of `spec`, I think less English is better way.

```suggestion
    *경로 작동*에 다양한 폼 매개변수를 선언할 수 있지만, 요청은 `application/json` 대신에 `application/x-www-form-urlencoded`을 사용하여 암호화 된 본문을 가지기 때문에, JSON으로 전달 받을 것이라 예상하는 `Body` 필드 또한 선언할 수 없습니다.
```

`동작` is for living things and `작동` is for kind of machines. Check *파이썬 공식 한국어 문서에 없는 용어 중 현재 사용하고 있는 용어* in #3167

title: Tiny doc change to improve clarity of Optional type hint note in tutorial
body:
When reading the documentation, the note regarding Optional type hints was somewhat confusing to me - it made it seem like FastAPI was ignoring relevant type information, which goes against all the rest of the beautiful type awareness. This is my attempt to clarify that note.

(Somewhat related to #1561, which seems as though it can be closed.)

title: ✨ Add support for `lifespan` async context managers (superseding `startup` and `shutdown` events)
body:
This adds support for the `lifespan` argument to the application and router, which was added in starlette.

I tidied up some of the event tests as well to promote reuse.

Fixes #2943

title: 🐛 Fix cached dependencies when using a dependency in `Security()` and other places (e.g. `Depends()`) with different OAuth2 scopes
body:
```python
class Dependant:
    def __init__(...):
        ...
        # Save the cache key at creation to optimize performance
        self.cache_key = (self.call, tuple(sorted(set(self.security_scopes or []))))
```

To make `.security_scopes` contribute to `.cache_key`, parameter `security_scopes` must be passed to `Dependant.__init__()`. We forgot to do it in this way. Reassignment after init `dependant_instance.security_scopes = ["dummy"]` doesn't change the `.cache_key` anymore. Main related logic is at `get_sub_dependant()`.

This makes `Security` with different scopes ([OAuth2 scopes][oauth2-scopes]) share the same cache. If we have `Security(func)` and `Security(func, scopes=['scope1'])` in a dependency tree. Only one of them will be really called.

The bug could be traced back to bff5dbb on 6/6/19 when the `dependency_cache` is first introduced. Seems a serious bug for anyone using `Security` in dependencies, better fix it soon.

[oauth2-scopes]: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/advanced/security/oauth2-scopes/

Hi guys,
it looks that changes from this PR broke my privs checking mechanism. 
Simplest code to demonstrate it is below:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends, Security
from fastapi.security import SecurityScopes


def get_obj(
    required_scopes: SecurityScopes,
):
    print(f'get_obj called with scopes: "{required_scopes.scope_str}"')
    return 'object'


def check_user_role_for_obj(
    required_scopes: SecurityScopes,
    obj=Depends(get_obj),
):
    print(f'check_user_role_for_obj called for {obj} with scopes:, "{required_scopes.scope_str}"')
    return 'object role'


app = FastAPI()


@app.get(
    '/',
    dependencies=[Security(check_user_role_for_obj, scopes=['read_scope'])],
)
def get_obj_with_scopes(
    obj=Depends(get_obj),
):
    return obj


@app.put(
    '/',
    dependencies=[Security(check_user_role_for_obj, scopes=['edit_scope'])],
)
def put_obj_with_scopes(
    obj=Depends(get_obj),
):
    return obj


@app.get(
    '/child_obj',
    dependencies=[Security(check_user_role_for_obj, scopes=['read_child_scope'])],
)
def get_obj_with_scopes():
    return 'child object'
```

Here is output from calling get_obj_with_scopes endpoint:
```
INFO:     127.0.0.1:58537 - "GET / HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
get_obj called with scopes: "read_scope"
check_user_role_for_obj called for object with scopes:, "read_scope"
get_obj called with scopes: ""
```
as you can see get_obj was called twice....
I understand that this change was necessary as I was also suprised that I can not use different scopes for the same dependencies, but thought that it is by desing ;]
Maybe my understanding of this is not correct, but in such case _get_obj_ itself is only used with _Depends_, so no specific scopes are requested for it, so maybe it should be cached?
Especially that I don't even need/use required_scopes: SecurityScopes in _get_obj_ function, so why differentiate cache key by it? Maybe it should depend if get_obj has SecurityScopes in its parameters list?

I am just throwing it for consideration.
As for now I will fix my FastAPI version to 0.79.1 and maybe I need to rethink how my privs model is designed.
Good Job guys!

Using a database session inside a `Security` and `Depends` also will open two sessions after this PR. Thus if the security method if returning a sqlalchemy the object return from the two methods will be attached to different sessions.

Hi @tiangolo,
maybe my case is kind specific to how I designed my privileges model, but creating multiple db sessions may lead to unexpected errors... I am not sure if I fully understand how dependency cache is working, but maybe it should be divided to 2 parts first scopes dependant and another not?
Can you please have a look on this?

To understand dependency cache, we may start from what features are added by FastAPI upon Starlette.

- When a request is received from the client at the server, it's received as bytes
- The ASGI server parse the bytes to `environ, recv(), send()` (ASGI standard)
- Starlette abstract the `environ` dict and `recv()` as a `Request`, which is a more developer-friendly Python object.

In the view/router function, we parse the `Request` (e.g. it's query param, json body, form body, etc), get the info we want, and compose a `Response` object.

`FastAPI` is capable to let developers declare the query param, or form, or json body as arguments of the view function. It helps us use the more finer part of `Request` directly without using the whole `Request`.

The introduction of dependency (`Depends`, `Security`) makes `FastAPI` more powerful. We can declare some composite value based on the primary data (query param, json body, form element, etc). e.g.

```python
async def sum_c(a: int = 0, b: int = 0):
    return a + b

@app.get("/sum")
async def calc_sum(c: int = Depends(sum_c)):
    return c
```

In the above example, the view func doesn't accept query param `a` or `b`, but a composite param named `c`, which is the sum of `a + b`.

To avoid duplicated, high cost calculation in some `Depends`, `FastAPI` caches the result. 

- For `Depends`, the cache key is based on the func wrapped by `Depends`
- For `Security` (subclass of `Depends`), the cache key is based on the wrapped func and param `scopes`. (Formerly `scopes` are ignored cause a bug in the implementation, and this PR fixed it.)

--- 

nparley qmorek ~~For your questions, you may separate db session initialization out of existing `Security`s as a standalone `Depends`, and re-used it as an argument in the existing multiple `Security`s.
This makes the new `Depends`, namely db session initialization process, has its own cache key, being executed only once, and not affected by `scopes` of `Security` anymore.~~ (Nope. I forgot that `scopes` is inherited in the dependency chain. Two `Depends` under two different `Security` have different cache key.)

title: Allow passing prefix trough application to internal router
body:
In some cases one want to set a global prefix, which easily is just passed to the internal router. This pull request allows code like the following:

```python

app = FastAPI(prefix='/api')


router = APIRouter(prefix='/users')


@app.get('/')  # /api/users/
def get_users():
    return ['Jake', 'Dave', 'Aly']


app.include_router(router)

```

The parameter name is up for discussion, I think something like `router_prefix` may get the wrong point across (like a default prefix for routers). So an ambigious `prefix` correctly gets the point of a global prefix for all routes correctly across.

One downside with this change is how it does what Nginx or Apache usually handles, and we probably want users to use those in many cases. But I think that this is easy enough of a change and should be available to the user.


You can currently use the following work-around until it this merged ( or rejected :/ ):

```python

class MyApp(FastAPI):
    if not typing.TYPE_CHECKING:  # For auto-complete
        def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
            super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
            self.router.prefix = "/api"

```

title: WIP: [Docs] - Fix OpenAPI version in extending openapi
body:
Hi,

Here is a small fix on the documentation.
As shown in this issue: tiangolo#1457 if we use openapi `2.5.0` as mentioned in the doc today we have a rendering problem. We need to use at least openapi `3.0.0` in order to get the Swagger UI displayed.

Have a good day.

PS: Thanks again for this framework ! It's just 👌
comments: 📝 Docs preview for commit 54f48c5ed74bac3be1f0c1698113b32478c072f2 at: https://604f9ca8c8d2e22cee398519--fastapi.netlify.app

Hey there! Thanks for the interest. This `version` is the version of your app, not of the OpenAPI specification. That one is `openapi_version`.

Given that I'll close this PR, but thanks for the interest! :coffee:

review_comments:
```suggestion
* 특정 경로에 `StaticFiles()` 인스턴스를 마운트 합니다.
```

how about this?  you already translate "mount" to "마운트"  in this docs
That's a good capture for me. Thank you very much.

```suggestion
"마운팅"은 특정 경로에 완전한 "독립적인" 애플리케이션을 추가하는 것을 말하는데, 그 후에는 모든 하위 경로에 대해서도 적용됩니다.
```

```suggestion
마운트된 애플리케이션은 완전히 독립적이기 때문에 `APIRouter`를 사용하는 것과는 다릅니다. OpenAPI 및 응용 프로그램의 문서는 마운트된 응용 프로그램 등에서 어떤 것도 포함하지 않습니다.
```
I think this section, **Install `aiofiles`** is deprecated. Please update (fetch) the files to the latest version.

```suggestion
* 특정 경로에 `StaticFiles()` 인스턴스를 "마운트" 합니다.
```

```suggestion
!!! note "기술적 세부사항"
```

Check *파이썬 공식 한국어 문서에 없는 용어 중 현재 사용하고 있는 용어* in #3167 .

```suggestion
    **FastAPI**는 단지 개발자인, 당신에게 편의를 제공하기 위해 `fastapi.static files` 와 동일한 `starlett.static files`를 제공합니다. 하지만 사실 이것은 Starlett에서 직접 온 것입니다.
```

```suggestion
마운트된 응용 프로그램은 완전히 독립적이기 때문에 `APIRouter`를 사용하는 것과는 다릅니다. OpenAPI 및 응용 프로그램의 문서는 마운트된 응용 프로그램 등에서 어떤 것도 포함하지 않습니다.
```

기존에 번역된 내용들과 일관성을 위해 지금은 유지하는게 좋을 것 같은데,
추후에 일괄적으로 변경하는 방향으로 진행하는 것은 어떨까요 ?
개인적으로 응용 프로그램이 주는 단어 느낌이, 데스크탑에서 사용하는 프로그램들이 더 익숙한 것 같기도 하네요.

[Korean translations](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/2017) 이슈의 📚 Glossary 에서는 다음 문서를 참고하라고 되어있네요! * https://docs.python.org/ko/3/glossary.html * https://www.flowdas.com/pages/python-docs-ko.html 위 링크에서 확인해보면 `application: 응용 프로그램` 으로 사용하고 있어서 응용프로그램으로 하는게 일관성 측면에서 좋을 것 같습니다^^ **이미지 출처: https://www.flowdas.com/pages/python-docs-ko.html > 용어집**

오 좋은 가이드와 함께 의견 감사합니다.
제안 반영 하겠습니다 👍

title: 📝 Add external link: Serve a machine learning model using Sklearn, FastAPI and Docker
body:
This pull requests adds the article "Serve a machine learning model using Sklearn, FastAPI and Docker" to the english external links documentation. 
The article itself includes an explanation and tutorial to use:

* FastAPI app with a machine learning model for online predictions
* Use of FastAPI with the Docker Image: tiangolo/uvicorn-gunicorn:python3.8-slim
* Load test using locust
* Use of auto generated docs (OpenAPI)
comments: Thank you @ArcLightSlavik for your feedback, I'm glad you enjoyed it, I'll keep it in mind and update it the tags to the APIRouter

review_comments: ```suggestion FastAPI фреймворк, высокая производительность, низкий порог входа, быстрая разработка, production-ready решение ```

```suggestion
FastAPI - это современный, высокопроизводительный веб-фреймворк для построения API на Python 3.6+, в основе которого лежит стандартная аннотация типов.
```

```suggestion
* **Скорость**: отличная производительность наравне с **NodeJS** и **Go** (благодаря Starlette и Pydantic). [Один из самых быстрых Python фреймворков](#performance).
```
Эффективность --- это не всегда скорость, т.е. эффективное действие может быть менее быстрое, но более экономичное, например.

```suggestion
* **Быстрая разработка**: увеличьте свою скорость разработки на 200–300%. *
```
```suggestion * **Соответствие стандартам**: Основан на открытых стандартах API: OpenAPI (ранее известный как Swagger) и JSON Schema, и полностью совместим с ними. ```
Альтернативное мнение.) ```suggestion Готовый к внедрению высокопроизводительный фреймворк, простой в изучении и разработке. ```

```suggestion
* **Лаконичность**: Минимальное дублирование кода. Большое количество готовых решений. Меньше багов.
```

title: Fix `response_model` typing
body:
Fix `response_model` typing when following documentation's `List[FooModel]` example:

[https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/extra-models/#list-of-models](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/extra-models/#list-of-models)

The documentation suggests something that conflicts with the typing, which is raised when using Pycharm:

> Expected type 'Optional[type]', got 'GenericAlias' instead 

This PR adds this type so warnings are not raised.

title: 📌 Pin SQLAlchemy range for tests, as it doesn't use SemVer
body:
📌 Pin SQLAlchemy range for tests, as it doesn't use SemVer and I forgot (or didn't know?) that when I pinned it the first time.

This only affects FastAPI internal tests... and by accident, [Pydantic tests](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/pull/2584). :grimacing: :see_no_evil:

title: Add include_in_schema parameter to headers to allow exclusion from OpenAPI spec
body:
Partially addresses #1708

Have the same use case as described by @mnorrsken 

```
We have a header with auth info thats always sent from our API manager to our backend. 
I want to consume this header in my API but I don't want it to show up in the docs.
```

title: ✨ Support Python internal description on Pydantic model's docstring
body:
## Problem

Currently, on FastAPI endpoint functions we can document for OpenAPI description/Swagger and for internal Python purposes:
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/")
def useful():
    """My description.
    \f
    Internal documentation.
    """
    ...
```
On Swagger we'll have:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7353520/113333626-f3008200-9322-11eb-9f9c-ca6ba5782d25.png)
 
When documenting models, it doesn't have the same behavior:
```python
from enum import Enum
from fastapi import FastAPI

class Banana(str, Enum):
    """My description.
    \f
    Internal documentation.
    """
    HAHA = "haha"
    HOHO = "hoho"

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/")
def useful(banana: Banana):
    ...
```
On Swagger, we'll have:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7353520/113333882-48d52a00-9323-11eb-8d0e-eef8cc965d04.png)
    
## Solution

The proposed _two lines of code_ on this PR, makes it possible to have the same behavior as the endpoint description:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7353520/113334003-6e623380-9323-11eb-849d-750a85f7847c.png)

It follows the same idea as: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/10397ddc30ef9bb26e9247f04fe20ce3045081c4/fastapi/routing.py#L350

## Related issues
- https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/3024

## Questions & Alternatives
- Should Pydantic handle this on the [model_process_schema()](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/blob/e68c277afe6bf6e7966bfecfe9b228d33f2de44b/pydantic/schema.py#L519) function instead?
comments: Do you guys have an opinion on this? @prettywood @samuelcolvin
